name: Cill ГѓВѓГ‚В‰inne (Killeany)
description: A low-lying monastic village near the bay, once described by George Petrie as a picturesque collection of thatched huts.
folklore: The spiritual capital of Aran, founded by St. Enda. Robinson notes the village was once a "colony" of the English garrison at Arkin. During the late 19th century, it was the site of intense sectarian strife. The "Soupers' List" (c. 1880s) contained ten families refused relief because they worked for the Protestant Reverend Kilbride or the shopkeeper Richard Charde.
nature: Coastal machair and sand dunes. Features the Wood Rush, rare on the islands.

name: Arkin Castle (Site of)
description: Ruins of a Cromwellian fortification built upon an older O'Flaherty stronghold.
folklore: Built during the reign of William III to defend against Dutch fishermen. Sir William Wilde in 1852 noted its massive walls. It remains a symbol of the "alien faith" and military presence that once dominated the harbor, with the village essentially serving the garrison's needs.
nature: Limestone foundations overlooking the sheltered, sea-splashed bay.

name: Tobar ГѓВѓГ‚В‰inne (St. Enda's Well)
description: A holy well dedicated to the islandГѓВўГ‚ВЂГ‚В™s patron, located south of the main village.
folklore: Still a vital site of pilgrimage. Local tradition holds the water never fails. Nearby, the faint traces of ClochГѓВѓГ‚ВЎn ГѓВѓГ‚В‰inne mark the site where the saint lived in ascetic seclusion. Informants like Beartla recall the persistence of rituals here even as modern life encroached.
nature: Karst pavement surrounding a natural spring; home to maiden-hair ferns.

name: An SuicГѓВѓГ‚В­n
description: A derelict area south of Joe WattyГѓВўГ‚ВЂГ‚В™s pub, formerly a crowded terrace of cottages.
folklore: Derived from a name for a "marshy patch" or "swallow-hole." This was the poorest quarter of Cill ГѓВѓГ‚В‰inne, where a hundred people once lived in a few long terraces. By the late 20th century, only two fragments of these one-room cottages remained, inhabited by a woman who famously disputed with the wind.
nature: Damp limestone depression with seasonal marsh characteristics.

name: An Jabaire (Jobber's Hill)
description: A steep ascent or carcair on the road leading west out of the village.
folklore: Named for "An Jabaire Beag" (the little jobber), Pat MullenГѓВўГ‚ВЂГ‚В™s uncle. Robinson describes the Jobber as a man five feet in height and ten stone, who carried a blackthorn stick in his left hand. The hill was a notorious site for faction fights where stick-fighting skills were tested against "the Morans."
nature: Rugged limestone steps and high drystone walls.

name: BГѓВѓГ‚ВіithrГѓВѓГ‚В­n na gCottageachaГѓВѓГ‚В­
description: A straight road lined with semi-detached cottages built by the CDB.
folklore: Constructed in 1915 by the Congested Districts Board to replace the "hovels" of the village. It represents the early 20th-century "kindness" of the British government (Home Rule by kindness) intended to pacify the peasantry through improved housing.
nature: A modern bisection of traditional small-field boundaries.

name: St. ThomasГѓВўГ‚ВЂГ‚В™s Church (Ruins)
description: An empty, roofless shell of a Protestant church near the rectory.
folklore: A stark reminder of seventy years of barren ministry. The roof was removed in 1921 to avoid paying rates, an act of practical necessity that nonetheless symbolized the final departure of the "alien" Protestant authority from the islandГѓВўГ‚ВЂГ‚В™s religious landscape.
nature: Limestone shell standing amidst a "sheltery backwater" of the town.

name: Digby House (Ruins)
description: The vanished three-story seat of the absentee Digby family.
folklore: Built by the Digbys, who acquired Aran in the 18th century. By 1845, the house was in ruins. Its stones were famously "cannibalized" to build the rectory and later the Catholic curateГѓВўГ‚ВЂГ‚В™s house, mirroring the shifting power dynamics of the islandГѓВўГ‚ВЂГ‚В™s landlords.
nature: Located behind a scarp, separated from the main Cill ГѓВѓГ‚В‰inne road.

name: Carcair na CГѓВѓГ‚В©artan (Forge Hill)
description: A steep rise named after a former smithy site.
folklore: Named for a forge run by Micil Riabhach ГѓВѓГ‚В“ Niaidh, described as a Connemara poitГѓВѓГ‚В­n smuggler who operated here a century ago. It marks the transition from the village to the higher limestone terraces of Eochaill.
nature: A classic carcair featuring sharp limestone "clints."

name: Joe WattyГѓВўГ‚ВЂГ‚В™s Pub
description: A local landmark at a turning in the road opposite the rectory.
folklore: Formerly an obscure public house run by Joe Watty, who died in 1975. Folklore tells that Joe would stand in prayer before St. RonanГѓВўГ‚ВЂГ‚В™s bed for twenty minutes every day, a testament to the deep, quiet piety that persisted alongside the villageГѓВўГ‚ВЂГ‚В™s social life.
nature: Sheltered by a row of cottages and a low limestone scarp.

name: The Rectory
description: A sturdy stone dwelling built for the Protestant minister.
folklore: Built using stones from the ruinous Digby House. It was the residence of Reverend Kilbride during the 1880s Land War, where he was famously besieged by the "Catholic faction" and once had a stone thrown through his window during the Ganly eviction dispute.
nature: Surrounded by treesГѓВўГ‚ВЂГ‚В”a rarity on the islandГѓВўГ‚ВЂГ‚В”and stone outbuildings.

name: Sulochan na Naomh
description: A small stone structure translated as "Seat of the Saints."
folklore: Found in the vicinity of Cill ГѓВѓГ‚В‰inne. Local tradition identifies this as a resting place or altar used by the early holy men who inhabited the monastic city under St. EndaГѓВўГ‚ВЂГ‚В™s rule.
nature: Weathered limestone block in a field of thin soil.

name: Teampall BheanГѓВѓГ‚ВЎin
description: A tiny, high-altitude oratory dedicated to St. Benan.
folklore: Cited as one of the smallest churches in Europe, it is oriented North-South rather than East-West. Benan was a disciple of St. Patrick. Its position on the ridge provided a lookout for the monastic community against sea-borne raiders.
nature: Perched on a high karst ridge; home to rose-beetles (Cetonia aurata).

name: Coastguard Station (Former)
description: A 19th-century government building near the pier.
folklore: Built to combat smuggling and monitor the coast. It represented the maritime arm of British administration. In the 1880s, the coastguards were often English Catholics who socialized with local families despite the political tensions.
nature: Stout stone masonry resistant to the corrosive salt air.

name: An Lurgain
description: A long, sloping ridge south of the village.
folklore: A topographical marker for the "long slope" that characterizes the transition from the sandy village to the interior crags. Used in local parlance to describe the grueling walk toward the western townlands.
nature: Characterized by thin grazing soil and limestone steps.

name: An Baile ThГѓВѓГ‚В­os
description: The "Lower Village" or coastal settlement.
folklore: Historically associated with the fishing families and the machair pastures. It was often flooded or "blown in" by shifting sands, which Petrie and O'Donovan noted during their 19th-century visits.
nature: Sandy coastal plain with marram grass stabilization.

name: ClochГѓВѓГ‚ВЎn ГѓВѓГ‚В‰inne
description: The beehive cell associated with St. Enda.
folklore: Located 200 yards south of Tobar ГѓВѓГ‚В‰inne. Only faint traces remain of this drystone corbelled structure where the saint practiced his most extreme forms of mortification.
nature: Surrounded by a "desert" of glaciated limestone pavement.

name: The 'Soupers' List' Site
description: The general vicinity of the village center near the old shops.
folklore: Folklore recounts the "theatrical interruption" during the 1880 investigation by John Adye Curran, where Mary Flaherty dragged her husband away to prevent him from revealing the sectarian relief list managed by Kilbride and Charde.
nature: A social rather than physical landmark, though centered near Charde's shop.

name: Dr. Stoney's Grave Site
description: A spot in the Protestant churchyard.
folklore: Dr. James Johnston Stoney, the dispensary doctor, lived a "tragi-comedy" of laudanum addiction and poverty. He was found dead in 1869. His grave remained unmarked until 1907, when his former driver, Peter Gill, returned from America to carve a stone for his master.
nature: Overgrown graveyard corner with small, unbaptized child-boulders.

name: An Chreig Dhubh (The Black Crag)
description: A vast, dark terrace of limestone pavement.
folklore: Noted for its "lugubrious" appearance. Local man Beartla commented that the Cill RГѓВѓГ‚ВіnГѓВѓГ‚ВЎin men were disgraced for not reclaiming this "wasteland" instead of spending their days lying under walls.
nature: Pure karst pavement; an "imbricated" landscape of deep grykes.

name: Creig Earraigh
description: A crag named for its specific character or owner.
folklore: Folklore associates this crag with the "Witty Aristotle," a figure in local tales who tricked a housekeeper into marriage and then cleverly outmaneuvered her during their separation by claiming the "three loads" he was promised.
nature: Sharp, glaciated limestone with minimal vegetation.

name: BГѓВѓГ‚ВіithrГѓВѓГ‚В­n an tSnГѓВѓГ‚ВЎmha
description: A small road leading to the swimming or launching area.
folklore: Used by the village families for access to the shore. It represents the functional "veins" of the townland, connecting the housing to the resources of the sea.
nature: Flanked by high, wind-breaking drystone walls.

name: An Currach Yard
description: The site near the harbor where boats are maintained.
folklore: The heart of AranГѓВўГ‚ВЂГ‚В™s maritime technology. Robinson notes the rapid growth of this area in the 1890s under CDB influence, transitioning from traditional skin boats to government-assisted designs.
nature: Near the pier; dominated by the smell of tar and salt.

name: Port na Cille
description: The "Harbor of the Church."
folklore: The ancient landing site for the monastic city. It was here that pilgrims would first step onto the islandГѓВўГ‚ВЂГ‚В™s "holy soil" after the crossing from Connemara.
nature: A natural limestone inlet with heavy sea-weed accumulation.

name: Leac na nГѓВѓГ‚В‰an
description: The "Ledge of the Birds."
folklore: A coastal ledge famous for its proximity to seabird colonies.
nature: Vertical limestone face; frequent sightings of guillemots and razorbills.

name: Poll na bPГѓВѓГ‚В©ist (The Worm Hole)
cluster: Barr Aille, The Southern Cliffs & Geological Wonders (Inishmore)
description: A perfectly rectangular natural pool cut into the limestone.
folklore: Known as the "Pool of the Sea-Monsters." Folklore suggests it was the lair of giant serpents. Geologically, it is a masterpiece of joint-intersection, where the sea has quarried out a massive block along vertical and horizontal lines.
nature: Sea-filled cavern with walls of tiered limestone beds.

name: An Aill Bhrisite (The Broken Cliff)
cluster: Barr Aille, The Southern Cliffs & Geological Wonders (Inishmore)
description: A vertical scarp or cliff-face along the road.
folklore: Features a "grotto" carved into a nook of the rock containing a Madonna. Robinson recounts seeing a donkey standing at the edge of this cliff, its ears "twiddling" against the Atlantic skyline.
nature: Nesting site for goldcrests; home to rambling roses and old man's beard.

name: An Aill MhГѓВѓГ‚Віr
cluster: Barr Aille, The Southern Cliffs & Geological Wonders (Inishmore)
description: The "Great Cliff" of the southern coast.
folklore: A massive wall of rock rising hundreds of feet above the Atlantic. It serves as the primary barrier against the southern swells that occasionally throw "imbricated" boulders onto the high terraces.
nature: Home to massive puffin colonies and fulmars.

name: An Chuinneog (The Churn)
cluster: Barr Aille, The Southern Cliffs & Geological Wonders (Inishmore)
description: A coastal rock formation or blow-hole.
folklore: Named for the "churning" sound and spray produced when Atlantic waves are forced through its narrow apertures during winter storms.
nature: A zone of extreme wave energy; polished limestone surfaces.

name: Carraig an PhГѓВѓГ‚В­obaire (Piper's Rock)
cluster: Barr Aille, The Southern Cliffs & Geological Wonders (Inishmore)
description: A distinctively shaped coastal outcrop.
folklore: Legend tells of an underground piper who entered the cave at An Uamhain and whose music could be heard beneath this rock as he traveled toward Inis MeГѓВѓГ‚ВЎin.
nature: Sharp limestone "clints" exposed to heavy salt spray.

name: Poll na gCaorach
cluster: Barr Aille, The Southern Cliffs & Geological Wonders (Inishmore)
description: The "Pool of the Sheep."
folklore: Historically used for washing sheep or perhaps where livestock were lost during the "twilight deeds" of the Land War, when animals were famously driven over cliffs.
nature: Sheltered coastal inlet with relatively calm water.

name: An Uamhain (The Small Cave)
cluster: Barr Aille, The Southern Cliffs & Geological Wonders (Inishmore)
description: A dark fissure or opening in the cliff base.
folklore: The entrance to the legendary tunnel used by the piper. It represents the islanders' belief in a subterranean world connecting the three islands.
nature: Dark, damp interior with maiden-hair ferns at the mouth.

name: Scailp an TГѓВѓГ‚ВЎiseir
cluster: Barr Aille, The Southern Cliffs & Geological Wonders (Inishmore)
description: A cleft named for a "ghost" or "vision."
folklore: Scailp refers to a deep fissure. This site is associated with coastal apparitions and the "tГѓВѓГ‚ВЎis" (ghosts) of those lost at sea.
nature: A deep, shadowed gryke near the cliff edge.

name: Poll BГѓВѓГ‚ВЎite
cluster: Barr Aille, The Southern Cliffs & Geological Wonders (Inishmore)
description: The "Drowning Pool."
folklore: A treacherous coastal spot. Robinson notes the "murderous futility" of the Atlantic here, which has claimed many islanders over the centuries.
nature: Churning, deep-water inlet with no easy egress.

name: An CorrГѓВѓГ‚ВЎn
cluster: Barr Aille, The Southern Cliffs & Geological Wonders (Inishmore)
description: A hook-shaped coastal feature.
folklore: Named for its "sickle" or "hook" shape. It is a landmark for fishermen navigating the treacherous southern shoreline.
nature: Jagged, wave-eroded limestone.

name: Port na Cille Cliffs
cluster: Barr Aille, The Southern Cliffs & Geological Wonders (Inishmore)
description: The cliffs overlooking the ancient monastic harbor.
folklore: These provided the first line of defense for Cill ГѓВѓГ‚В‰inne. O'Donovan and Wakeman noted the ruins of Arkin were built to defend these waters from Dutch raiders.
nature: Limestone scarps with high concentrations of maritime lichens.

name: Buaile na gCopГѓВѓГ‚Віg
cluster: Barr Aille, The Southern Cliffs & Geological Wonders (Inishmore)
description: The "Pasture of the Plantains."
folklore: A small ledge of ground between the road and the Aill Bhrisite scarp. Robinson calls it the "last wedge of ground" before the ascent to Eochaill.
nature: A rare fertile pocket; home to plantains and Wood Rush.

name: An Aill Droichead
cluster: Barr Aille, The Southern Cliffs & Geological Wonders (Inishmore)
description: The "Bridge Cliff."
folklore: A natural limestone arch where the sea has eroded the lower beds, leaving a span of rock above.
nature: Spectacular geomorphological feature; dangerous in high winds.

name: An tSreang
cluster: Barr Aille, The Southern Cliffs & Geological Wonders (Inishmore)
description: The "String" or "Line."
folklore: A narrow, linear ledge along the cliff face used by daring "cliff-men" to gather eggs or hunt birds in previous centuries.
nature: Precarious limestone shelf; home to shags and cormorants.

name: Poll na bPoncГѓВѓГ‚ВЎn
cluster: Barr Aille, The Southern Cliffs & Geological Wonders (Inishmore)
description: A pool associated with "Yankees" or returned emigrants.
folklore: Folklore suggests it was a favorite spot for those who returned from America with "pampooties replaced by boots" to look out at the sea they once crossed.
nature: A scenic lookout point over the Atlantic.

name: An Buaile Ghlas Cliffs
cluster: Barr Aille, The Southern Cliffs & Geological Wonders (Inishmore)
description: The cliffs near the "Green Pasture."
folklore: Marks the point where the verdant interior fields meet the stark verticality of the southern coast.
nature: Transition zone from machair to vertical karst.

name: An PhailГѓВѓГ‚В­s
cluster: Barr Aille, The Southern Cliffs & Geological Wonders (Inishmore)
description: The "Palace."
folklore: Sarcastic local name for a particularly grand-looking natural rock shelter or a well-built drystone "booley" hut used by herdsmen.
nature: Hidden limestone nook; provides shelter from Atlantic gales.

name: An Scailp MhГѓВѓГ‚Віr
cluster: Barr Aille, The Southern Cliffs & Geological Wonders (Inishmore)
description: The "Great Fissure."
folklore: A massive crack in the limestone pavement near the cliff edge, deep enough to hide a man.
nature: Micro-climate for ferns and mosses; protected from the wind.

name: Ceann na bhFaochanai
cluster: Barr Aille, The Southern Cliffs & Geological Wonders (Inishmore)
description: The "Head of the Periwinkles."
folklore: A major foraging site where island women would gather shellfish from the rock pools at low tide.
nature: Wide intertidal limestone shelves with deep grykes.

name: An SundaГѓВѓГ‚Ві (The South Sound)
cluster: Barr Aille, The Southern Cliffs & Geological Wonders (Inishmore)
description: The stretch of water between Inishmore and the southern isles.
folklore: Robinson describes the "terrors of a Vesuvian eruption" that must have torn these islands apart, creating this deep sound.
nature: Deep Atlantic channel with heavy currents and grey seals.

name: Mainistir Farm
cluster: Mainistir, Gort na gCapall & The Interior (Inishmore)
description: A fertile farmstead near the ancient monastic ruins.
folklore: The "ground zero" of the Land War on Aran. In 1879, Pat Ganly and his mother were evicted from here. The farm was then given to Reverend Kilbride, inciting a decade of "twilight deeds," including the stabbing of ChardeГѓВўГ‚ВЂГ‚В™s cattle and the burning of the rent office.
nature: Deep soil compared to the surrounding karst; good for rye and potatoes.

name: Leaba RГѓВѓГ‚ВіnГѓВѓГ‚ВЎin (St. Ronan's Bed)
cluster: Mainistir, Gort na gCapall & The Interior (Inishmore)
description: A square plot delimited by a low wall in the Mainistir graveyard.
folklore: Site of the "sleep-cure" for lameness on August 15th. In 1947, Fr. Killeen cleared the area of elderberry bushes to allow processions. Local man Dara the Postman recalled the superstition that cutting the "limb of St. Ronan" (the elder tree) would bring a stroke.
nature: Enclosure with a cross-inscribed stone; shaded by elderberry.

name: Log na Marbh (Hollow of the Dead)
cluster: Mainistir, Gort na gCapall & The Interior (Inishmore)
description: A squarish mound with stone kerbing, "a couple of hundred yards below the road, by a modern water-tank."
folklore: Site of the 1584 massacre. Nine Connemara men, led by sons of the seneschal of Clann Maurice, were slaughtered here by Tadhg na Buile O'Flaherty after a nocturnal expedition during the O'Flaherty/Murchadh clan wars.
nature: Sandy pasturage half a mile from the seashore.

name: Gort na gCapall
cluster: Mainistir, Gort na gCapall & The Interior (Inishmore)
description: The "Field of the Horses" townland.
folklore: Birthplace of Liam O'Flaherty. During the 1920 Black and Tan raids, Thomas Fleming and other Volunteers drilled on these roads, much to the annoyance of "Johnston himself," a local supporter of the Crown.
nature: Characterized by high stone walls and imbricated karst boulders.

name: Hill Farm
cluster: Mainistir, Gort na gCapall & The Interior (Inishmore)
description: A farmstead in the interior highlands.
folklore: A hiding place for three Galway Volunteers in 1920. They spent weeks in a store here, protected by the "comprehensive loyalty" of the islanders, who refused to betray them even during intensive Black and Tan searches.
nature: High-point grazing land; exposed to the Atlantic winds.

name: Creig na Fola
cluster: Mainistir, Gort na gCapall & The Interior (Inishmore)
description: The "Crag of the Blood."
folklore: Named for a violent incident, possibly during the Land War or an older clan skirmish. It remains a "grim" landmark in the island's interior.
nature: Dark, weathered limestone pavement with deep grykes.

name: Aill Chreig an TГѓВѓГ‚В­
cluster: Mainistir, Gort na gCapall & The Interior (Inishmore)
description: The "Cliff of the House Crag."
folklore: A topographical landmark within the interior fields, marking a sharp drop in the limestone terraces.
nature: Features Cetonia aurata (rose-beetles) in the summer months.

name: Log an tSnГѓВѓГ‚ВЎmha
cluster: Mainistir, Gort na gCapall & The Interior (Inishmore)
description: The "Hollow of the Swimming."
folklore: An interior depression that collects water, or a path leading to a safe coastal pool.
nature: Seasonal ponding area; home to specialized karst flora.

name: An Charraig ChГѓВѓГ‚Вєl
cluster: Mainistir, Gort na gCapall & The Interior (Inishmore)
description: The "Back Rock."
folklore: A massive erratic boulder or outcrop used as a boundary marker between the interior townlands.
nature: Glacial erratic resting on "clints" of limestone.

name: Creig an BhobailГѓВѓГ‚В­n
cluster: Mainistir, Gort na gCapall & The Interior (Inishmore)
description: A crag named for a specific local family or character.
folklore: A traditional landmark in the interior, used for navigating the labyrinth of stone walls that Robinson famously mapped.
nature: High-relief karst; home to maiden-hair ferns.

name: An ScreigГѓВѓГ‚В­n
cluster: Mainistir, Gort na gCapall & The Interior (Inishmore)
description: The "Small Stony Place."
folklore: A characteristic patch of loose limestone scree.
nature: Arid, rocky ground with minimal soil; home to Meligethes rufipes.

name: Buaile na gCopГѓВѓГ‚Віg
cluster: Mainistir, Gort na gCapall & The Interior (Inishmore)
description: The "Pasture of the Docks/Plantains."
folklore: A high pasture used for summer grazing. Its name reflects the presence of Rumex (docks) which thrive in the pockets of deeper soil.
nature: Relatively lush grass compared to the surrounding "desert."

name: An Pholl BhГѓВѓГ‚ВЎite (Interior)
cluster: Mainistir, Gort na gCapall & The Interior (Inishmore)
description: An inland "Drowning Pool" or deep swallow-hole.
folklore: Folklore warns children to stay away from this "bottomless" hole, which is said to lead directly to the sea.
nature: A classic karst doline or swallow-hole.

name: Creig na hUanfaileann
cluster: Mainistir, Gort na gCapall & The Interior (Inishmore)
description: The "Crag of the Seagulls."
folklore: An inland crag where gulls are known to rest away from the coastal winds.
nature: High limestone terrace; often stained with white guano.

name: An Lorgain (Interior)
cluster: Mainistir, Gort na gCapall & The Interior (Inishmore)
description: The inland portion of the "Long Slope."
folklore: Represents the grueling central ridge of the island, where the "Stones of Aran" are at their most dense and unyielding.
nature: Continuous limestone pavement with very few gryke plants.

name: BГѓВѓГ‚ВіithrГѓВѓГ‚В­n na gCottageachaГѓВѓГ‚В­ (West)
cluster: Mainistir, Gort na gCapall & The Interior (Inishmore)
description: The western extension of the CDB road.
folklore: A physical marker of the 1915 modernization. Robinson notes the contrast between these straight roads and the "twisty boreens" of the old villages.
nature: Flanked by modern drystone walling.

name: Creig Dhubh (Mainistir)
cluster: Mainistir, Gort na gCapall & The Interior (Inishmore)
description: The "Black Crag" near the monastery.
folklore: Described as "lugubrious" by Robinson. It was across this desolate landscape that the Land Leaguers would move at night to perform their "twilight deeds."
nature: Dark, glaciated limestone; visually desolate.

name: An Jabaire Beag's Cottage
cluster: Mainistir, Gort na gCapall & The Interior (Inishmore)
description: The site of the Jobber's house.
folklore: A "meeting-place for the man from the east and the man from the west." It was here that faction fights were planned and stick-fighting lore was passed down.
nature: Small stone ruins near the Carcair an Jabaire.

name: Mainistir Burial Ground
cluster: Mainistir, Gort na gCapall & The Interior (Inishmore)
description: The ancient cemetery serving the southern townlands.
folklore: Contains the "Leaba RГѓВѓГ‚ВіnГѓВѓГ‚ВЎin." Robinson notes it was a "sheltery backwater" where people slept on the eve of the saint's day to seek cures.
nature: Shaded by elderberry and hawthorn; home to the flower-beetle.

name: An Gleann (Interior)
cluster: Mainistir, Gort na gCapall & The Interior (Inishmore)
description: The "Valley" depression.
folklore: One of the few areas with enough shelter and soil to sustain small trees or larger shrubs.
nature: Micro-climate protected from the Atlantic salt-spray.

name: CreigГѓВѓГ‚ВЎn Ard
cluster: Mainistir, Gort na gCapall & The Interior (Inishmore)
description: The "High Small Crag."
folklore: A prominent outcrop used by the Volunteers in 1920 to keep watch for the "man-of-war" in the bay and the landing of the Black and Tans.
nature: Strategic high point; panoramic views of the North Sound.

name: BГѓВѓГ‚ВіithrГѓВѓГ‚В­n na bPampooties
cluster: Mainistir, Gort na gCapall & The Interior (Inishmore)
description: A path named for the traditional hide shoes.
folklore: Used by those traveling to the "back" of the island to work the kelp-kilns or hunt birds.
nature: Worn limestone path, polished by generations of pampooties.

name: An Seana-Bhaile
cluster: Mainistir, Gort na gCapall & The Interior (Inishmore)
description: The "Old Village" site.
folklore: Traces of abandoned dwellings from the pre-Famine era, when the population was much denser.
nature: Stone footings overgrown with brambles and ivy.

name: Poll an RГѓВѓГ‚ВіidГѓВѓГ‚В­n
cluster: Mainistir, Gort na gCapall & The Interior (Inishmore)
description: The "Pool of the Little Road."
folklore: A landmark at the end of a long boithrГѓВѓГ‚В­n leading to the sea.
nature: Coastal inlet; home to grey seals.

name: Cnocan na gCapall
cluster: Mainistir, Gort na gCapall & The Interior (Inishmore)
description: The "Small Hill of the Horses."
folklore: A rise in Gort na gCapall where livestock were gathered for the "horse-dealing" that Joe Watty and his neighbors famously engaged in.
nature: Gentle limestone rise with thin grass.

name: DГѓВѓГ‚Вєn Formna
description: An irregular cashel wall on the islandГѓВўГ‚ВЂГ‚В™s highest knoll.
folklore: Formna means the ridge or shoulder of a hill. The site contains a 14th-century O'Brien tower-house, destroyed by Cromwellians in 1652. It represents the ancient defensive spine of the southern isle.
nature: Built on a limestone knoll; home to maritime lichens.

name: Teampall ChaomhГѓВѓГ‚ВЎin
description: A 10th-century church, famous for being buried by shifting sands.
folklore: Dedicated to St. CaomhГѓВѓГ‚ВЎn, brother of St. Kevin of Glendalough. Shifting dunes bury the church so frequently that it must be dug out for the saintГѓВўГ‚ВЂГ‚В™s feast day (June 14th).
nature: Surrounded by a kitchen midden of ancient seashells.

name: CaisleГѓВѓГ‚ВЎn UГѓВѓГ‚В­ Bhriain
description: The tower-house within DГѓВѓГ‚Вєn Formna.
folklore: The stronghold of the O'Briens, who ruled the islands until the late 16th century. Its ruin stands as a testament to the O'Flaherty/O'Brien conflicts for control of the Atlantic trade routes.
nature: Massive drystone and mortar masonry.

name: Cill Ghobnait
description: A small 8th-century church dedicated to St. Gobnet.
folklore: Features a narrow lintelled West door and a tiny East window. Nearby are early graves and the remains of a clochГѓВѓГ‚ВЎn.
nature: Situated in a quiet limestone depression.

name: Cnoc RaithnГѓВѓГ‚В­
description: A Bronze-Age tumulus (c. 1600-1400 B.C.).
folklore: Translated as the "Hill of Ferns." In 1885, shifting sands uncovered two urn-burials, providing evidence of prehistoric habitation on the island.
nature: A mound of sand and stone near the shore.

name: Inisheer Lighthouse
description: A tall stone tower built in 1857.
folklore: Automated in 1978. Its construction brought "outsiders" to the island, including the smithy TГѓВѓГ‚В­ Smith, influencing the local social structure.
nature: Overlooking the An SundaГѓВѓГ‚Ві (South Sound).

name: The 'Plassy'
description: A freighter wrecked on the eastern coast in 1960.
folklore: Wrecked on Carraig na Finnise. Storms have since lifted the hull five feet higher than high tide, creating a landmark of rusting iron against the limestone.
nature: Resting on a storm beach of massive limestone boulders.

name: Cathair na mBan
description: A ruinous stone fort translated as "Fort of the Women."
folklore: Now a circular mound of loose limestone rubble. Local legend suggests it was a place of refuge for women during raids.
nature: A hilltop cairn-like structure of loose rock.

name: Reilig PГѓВѓГ‚Віil
description: The traditional site of a buried church dedicated to St. Paul.
folklore: Marked only by a few stones in a field. It reflects the many "lost" religious sites of the southern isle.
nature: Overgrown with wild grasses and mosses.

name: An Uamhain (Inis OГѓВѓГ‚В­rr)
description: A small cave with a legendary connection to Inishmore.
folklore: Folklore tells of a piper who entered here and emerged in Inis MeГѓВѓГ‚ВЎin; his music was said to be heard under Carraig an PhГѓВѓГ‚В­obaire.
nature: Dark opening in the karst pavement.

name: Buaile Glas
description: The "Green Pasture."
folklore: Features traces of a surrounding wall; "Glas" refers to its perennial green color amidst the gray rock.
nature: A verdant pocket of machair grass.

name: GarraГѓВѓГ‚В­ an DiГѓВѓГ‚ВєidГѓВѓГ‚В­n
description: Garden of the Pipe.
folklore: Contains a small rectangular structure which some believe might be a megalithic tomb.
nature: A walled enclosure with deeper soil.

name: Cill na Seacht nInГѓВѓГ‚В­on
description: Church of the Seven Daughters.
folklore: Remains of an old monastic foundation with a cashel wall, altar, and cross-inscribed stones.
nature: Remote eastern site; very peaceful.

images.author: louise price
images.source: https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Cill na Seacht NiniГѓВѓГ‚Віn - Inis OГѓВѓГ‚В­rr - geograph.org.uk - 3553806.jpg

name: Kelp Kiln
description: A rectangular stone structure built by the government in the 1930s.
folklore: Used to produce iodine from kelp. It represents the brief industrialization of the traditional seaweed harvest.
nature: Low, functional stone masonry.

name: Seaweed Stands
description: Circular stone stands for drying ricks of weed.
folklore: Built by the CDB to assist the local kelp industry. These "sea-rod" drying stations are a unique feature of the Inis OГѓВѓГ‚В­rr landscape.
nature: Short lengths of wall and circular platforms.

name: TГѓВѓГ‚В­ Smith
description: A former smithy used during the lighthouse construction.
folklore: A functional relic of the mid-19th century industrial presence on the island.
nature: Small, sturdy stone building near the lighthouse.

name: Baile an tSГѓВѓГ‚В©ipГѓВѓГ‚В©il
description: The "Village of the Chapel."
folklore: The primary settlement center, named for its proximity to the religious heart of the island.
nature: Cluster of traditional and modern dwellings.

name: An Bhluibh
description: An obscurely named field or landmark.
folklore: A traditional topographical marker in the Baile an tSГѓВѓГ‚В©ipГѓВѓГ‚В©il area.
nature: Small grassy enclosure near the village.

name: Gleann an Fhormna
description: The "Valley of the Ridge."
folklore: A depression between the central ridges of the island.
nature: Relatively sheltered valley with better soil for grazing.

name: Ceann na Faolchanai
description: The "Headland of the Periwinkles."
folklore: A coastal point known for shellfish gathering and foraging.
nature: Jagged limestone rocks at the water's edge.

name: Scalp Bhrady
description: BradyГѓВўГ‚ВЂГ‚В™s Cleft.
folklore: A specific fissure in the limestone cliffs named for a local figure.
nature: Narrow gap in the coastal rock face.

name: UlГѓВѓГ‚ВЎn Dubh
description: The "Black Shoulder."
folklore: A dark rocky prominence on the coast, providing a landmark for sailors.
nature: High-contrast limestone prominence.

name: Cois Caol
description: The "Narrow Side."
folklore: A slender strip of land near the shore, historically used for grazing.
nature: Thin grazing land bordering the sea.

name: BullГѓВѓГ‚ВЎn an Chapaill
description: The "Horse's Stone Basin."
folklore: A bullГѓВѓГ‚ВЎn or man-made depression in a rock, likely used for ritual or livestock watering.
nature: Large boulder with a deep, circular hollow.

name: BГѓВѓГ‚В©al an Chalaidh
description: The "Mouth of the Landing Place."
folklore: A gap or entry point in the landscape near the traditional harbor.
nature: A rocky passage between field walls.

name: Leic an ChaisleГѓВѓГ‚ВЎin
description: The "Castle Ledge."
folklore: A flat area of rock supporting the lower defenses of the O'Brien stronghold.
nature: Broad limestone pavement.

name: DГѓВѓГ‚Вєn na NГѓВѓГ‚В­
description: The "Fort of the Daughter."
folklore: A small ancient enclosure perched on the cliff edge.
nature: Partially collapsed stone wall; spectacular views.

name: TrГѓВѓГ‚ВЎcht Mil
description: Honey Strand.
folklore: A beach noted for its fine, golden sand. Robinson notes the beauty of these small coves.
nature: Sheltered cove with white sand.

name: Leic MhГѓВѓГ‚Віr
description: The "Great Ledge."
folklore: A massive horizontal shelf of limestone.
nature: One of the largest single pavements on Inis OГѓВѓГ‚В­rr.

name: Gob an BhorrГѓВѓГ‚ВЎin
description: The "Point of the Hump."
folklore: A protruding piece of land on the coast.
nature: Sharp, wave-eroded limestone.

name: Furdoras
description: The "Outer Door."
folklore: A natural gap or "gate" in the coastal rocks.
nature: Narrow sea-passage between cliffs.

name: Loig BhuГѓВѓГ‚В­
description: The "Yellow Hollow."
folklore: Likely named for the color of the clay or specific wildflowers in the depression.
nature: A dip in the landscape with unique flora.

name: Poll Loige BuГѓВѓГ‚В­
description: The "Pool of the Yellow Hollow."
folklore: A coastal pool near the Loig BhuГѓВѓГ‚В­ depression.
nature: Deep seawater pool.

name: Cloch Chormaic
description: CormacГѓВўГ‚ВЂГ‚В™s Stone.
folklore: A prominent erratic boulder or memorial stone in the open fields.
nature: Prominent glacial erratic.

name: Poll DhГѓВѓГ‚Віnaill
description: DonalГѓВўГ‚ВЂГ‚В™s Pool.
folklore: A coastal pool named after a local fisherman or historical figure.
nature: Rocky inlet with deep water.

name: Log na Marbh
description: The Hollow of the Dead, situated in low-lying sandy pasturage north-west of Baile an DГѓВѓГ‚Вєin. It contains a squarish mound with stone kerbing and a small upright stone.
folklore: Site of the last outbreak of clan warfare in Iar-Chonnacht (1584). A contention arose between the descendants of Owen and Murchadh OГѓВўГ‚ВЂГ‚В™Flaherty over the island's possession. Nine Connemara men, part of a nocturnal expedition, were slaughtered here. Nearby, a plaque commemorates Lawrence Mc Donogh, shot by the Black and Tans on December 19, 1920.
nature: Sandy pasturage subject to winter rain storms; the "hollow" serves as a natural sink for runoff in the coastal dunes.

name: An SuicГѓВѓГ‚В­n
description: A derelict quarter located behind Joe WattyГѓВўГ‚ВЂГ‚В™s pub, once a populous terrace of one-room thatched cottages. The derivation likely refers to a marshy patch or "swallow-hole."
folklore: This was once the poorest quarter of the village where a hundred people lived in cramped conditions. Robinson notes an eerie atmosphere where a womanГѓВўГ‚ВЂГ‚В™s voice was heardГѓВўГ‚ВЂГ‚В”a "disembodied voice" representing the "accumulated pangs of female life" from generations of hardship.
nature: A solutional cavity in the karst limestone that once collected moisture in an otherwise dry terrain.

name: An Chreig Dhubh
description: The Black Crag, a massive terrace of limestone pavement in the higher townlands, known for its austere, unyielding surface.
folklore: Old Beartla considered this crag a "disgrace" to the island because it remained unreclaimed wasteland. He famously compared the "cleverness" of the rock to "Harry Stattle" (Aristotle), a figure of local folklore who was said to have outwitted a housekeeper in a marriage trial.
nature: Characterized by deep crevices or grykes and weathered limestone pavement.

name: Teampall ChaomhГѓВѓГ‚ВЎn
description: A 10th-century church dedicated to St. CaomhГѓВѓГ‚ВЎn, brother of St. Kevin of Glendalough. The nave is a later Medieval addition.
folklore: Partially buried by drifting sands for centuries, the church required constant excavation. St. CaomhГѓВѓГ‚ВЎn is a central figure in island hagiography; a huge kitchen midden of seashells nearby dates back to Early Christian times, suggesting long-term ritual habitation.
nature: Set within coastal dunes; the surrounding sand-system is stabilized by marram grass.

name: Cill Ghobnait
description: A small, early (8th or 9th century) church dedicated to St. Gobnet of Ballyvourney. Features a narrow lintelled West door.
folklore: Located near Early Historic graves and the remains of a clochГѓВѓГ‚ВЎn (stone cell). It represents the spread of the Cult of Gobnet from the mainland to the islands during the monastic expansion.
nature: Surrounded by limestone terraces and early botanical remnants of monastic gardening.

name: Leaba RГѓВѓГ‚ВіnГѓВѓГ‚ВЎin
description: St. RГѓВѓГ‚ВіnГѓВѓГ‚ВЎnГѓВўГ‚ВЂГ‚В™s "bed" or grave; a four-yard square plot delimited by a low wall with a small altar and cross-inscribed stone.
folklore: Local tradition held that sleeping in the leaba on the eve of the saint's day (August 15) provided cures. Robinson notes that even in the 20th century, crutches were left behind by those healed through these clifftop vigils.
nature: Features a solitary elderberry bush, once uprooted by an Englishman who was allegedly "smitten with a stroke" for his sacrilege.

name: An Aill Bhriste
description: The Broken Cliff, a vertical rock-face along the road rising from the village toward the interior.
folklore: A landmark on the "long ascent" that islanders faced when returning from the harbor. It signifies the transition from the sheltered village to the exposed, rocky "backwaters" of the island.
nature: Features a "grotto" in a nook of the cliff with a conventional blue and white Madonna, surrounded by rambling roses and ivy.

name: Carcair an Atharla
description: A steep limestone slope or scarp leading down to the burial ground of Baile an DГѓВѓГ‚Вєin.
folklore: The primary route for funeral processions. It is where the community pauses before descending to the grave of St. RГѓВѓГ‚ВіnГѓВѓГ‚ВЎn, marking a boundary between the world of the living and the dead.
nature: A significant geological scarp separating the upper limestone terraces from the lower sandy hamlets.

name: Carcair an Jabaire
description: JobberГѓВўГ‚ВЂГ‚В™s Hill, named after the uncle of Pat Mullen (author of Hero Breed), a cattle dealer or "jobber."
folklore: A traditional site for faction fights. The "Little Jobber," a man of five feet height but immense strength, was a legendary stick-fighter who practiced his "bauneen" wrapping techniques here to ward off blows from the Morans.
nature: A steep roadway rising out of the village, showcasing the terraced nature of the karst landscape.

name: Carcair na CГѓВѓГ‚В©artan
description: The Scarp of the Forge, where a blacksmithГѓВўГ‚ВЂГ‚В™s shop once stood on the right of the road.
folklore: The forge was operated by Micil Riabhach ГѓВѓГ‚В“ Niaidh, a Connemara poitГѓВѓГ‚В­n smuggler who lived a century ago. It represents the island's historical reliance on clandestine maritime trade.
nature: Marks the entry point into the higher limestone pavement areas.

name: Cnoc RaithnГѓВѓГ‚В­
description: Hill of Ferns, a Bronze-Age tumulus (c. 1600-1400 B.C.) located in the sandy dunes.
folklore: In 1885, as the sands shifted, two urn-burials were uncovered. This discovery provided the first archaeological proof of prehistoric habitation on the island, coinciding with a period of intense famine relief works.
nature: A mound of ferns and sand; Robinson notes the presence of the "rose-beetle" (Cetonia aurata) in this vicinity.

name: Cill na Seacht nInГѓВѓГ‚В­on
description: Church of the Seven Daughters, the remains of an old monastic foundation with a cashel wall and altar.
folklore: An ecclesiastical site of high significance; foundations of multiple buildings and cross-inscribed stones remain. It is the spiritual counterpart to the Seven Churches of Inishmore, highlighting the archipelago's monastic network.
nature: Nestled within ancient stone enclosures that protect specialized karst flora from grazing.

images.author: louise price
images.source: https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Cill na Seacht NiniГѓВѓГ‚Віn - Inis OГѓВѓГ‚В­rr - geograph.org.uk - 3553806.jpg

name: DГѓВѓГ‚Вєn Formna
description: A prehistoric stone fort or cashel wall following the lines of the knoll upon which it stands.
folklore: Formna signifies the "ridge" or "shoulder" of a hill. It represents the earliest defensive architecture on Inis OГѓВѓГ‚В­rr, predating the O'Brien tower-house built within its perimeter.
nature: Built on the highest ridge of the island, offering a vantage over the South Sound.

name: CaisleГѓВѓГ‚ВЎn UГѓВѓГ‚В­ Bhriain
description: A 14th-century tower-house built within the walls of DГѓВѓГ‚Вєn Formna by the O'Briens.
folklore: Occupied by the O'Briens until 1582 and later destroyed by Cromwellian forces in 1652. It is the most prominent landmark on the islandГѓВўГ‚ВЂГ‚В™s skyline, symbolizing Gaelic lordship.
nature: Constructed from local limestone, it is now a shell integrated into the prehistoric fortГѓВўГ‚ВЂГ‚В™s fabric.

name: The Plassy
description: A freighter wrecked on the rocks in 1960.
folklore: Successive Atlantic storms have lifted the vessel five feet higher than normal high tides. It has become a modern folkloric landmark and a testament to the power of the sea on the Carraig na Finnise reef.
nature: Rusted iron hull sitting atop the rocky storm beach, now part of the island's maritime ecology.

images.author: Harold Strong
images.source: https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:On the Rocks, Inisheer, Aran Islands -2- - geograph.org.uk - 637547.jpg

name: Inisheer Lighthouse
description: A navigational tower built in 1857 and automated in 1978.
folklore: Associated with the 1857 visit of the British Association, whose members, led by William Wilde, were pioneers in island ethnography. It marks the modernized maritime boundary of the island.
nature: Situated on the "back" of the island, facing the South Sound and the Clare coast.

name: TГѓВѓГ‚В­ Smith
description: A smithy used during the construction of the lighthouse in 1857.
folklore: An industrial archaeological site from the Victorian era, representing a brief influx of mainland labor and technology.
nature: Stone building ruins near the lighthouse, weathered by salt spray.

name: An Uamhain
description: A small cave near the shoreline.
folklore: Legend tells of a piper who entered this cave and was never seen again; however, his music was reportedly heard emanating from under Carraig an PhГѓВѓГ‚В­obaire in Inis MeГѓВѓГ‚ВЎin.
nature: A natural solution cavity within the limestone, typical of karst topography.

name: Cathair na mBan
description: Fort of the Women, a ruinous stone enclosure.
folklore: Now appearing as little more than a hilltop cairn, it is associated with feminine ritual space or female-led defensive efforts in oral tradition.
nature: A highly eroded archaeological site located on the exposed heights.

name: SuГѓВѓГ‚В­ochГѓВѓГ‚ВЎn na Naomh
description: Seat of the Saints, an ancient stone structure resembling an altar.
folklore: A ritual site where islanders and pilgrims would rest. It is part of the sacred geography connecting the various monastic ruins.
nature: A structured arrangement of limestone blocks in the interior.

name: Reilig PГѓВѓГ‚Віil
description: The traditional site of a buried church dedicated to St. Paul.
folklore: Only a few stones remain to mark the site. It is one of the "lost" churches of the island, often discussed by antiquarians like Petrie and O'Donovan.
nature: Buried under shifting sands and encroaching vegetation.

name: Buaile Glas
description: Green Pasture, a burial place with a trace of a surrounding wall.
folklore: A consecrated or traditional burial ground used when larger cemeteries were inaccessible or for specific ritual groups.
nature: A rare patch of verdant grass amidst the limestone pavement.

name: GarraГѓВѓГ‚В­ an DiГѓВѓГ‚ВєidГѓВѓГ‚В­n
description: Garden of the Pipe, containing a small rectangular stone structure.
folklore: Archaeologists suggest it may be a megalithic tomb; locally, it is viewed as an unusual enclosure related to agricultural history.
nature: Stone-walled garden with deep soil, a rarity in the "backwaters."

name: Tobar ГѓВѓГ‚В‰inne
description: St. EndaГѓВўГ‚ВЂГ‚В™s Well, a site of active pilgrimage.
folklore: Still visited for its curative waters. Robinson notes that while ClochГѓВѓГ‚ВЎn ГѓВѓГ‚В‰inne is nearby, only a trace of the saint's cell remains.
nature: A natural spring issuing through a fissure in the limestone.

name: An PhГѓВѓГ‚Вєca
description: The Pooka, a short-cut road or boreen.
folklore: Named after the mischievous spirit of Irish folklore; the road is said to be haunted, particularly during the winter months.
nature: A narrow, winding boreen flanked by high stone walls.

name: BГѓВѓГ‚ВіithrГѓВѓГ‚В­n na gCottageachaГѓВѓГ‚В­
description: CDB-Irish road, built by the Congested Districts Board.
folklore: Constructed in 1915 to replace the "hovels" and "disgraceful" dwellings of the poor with modern stone cottages.
nature: Represents the first planned 20th-century housing development on the island.

name: An Lui MhГѓВѓГ‚Віr
description: The Big Lie-Down, a field in the village of Mainistir.
folklore: Named after a large horse nicknamed "The General" that would roll in the grass after carting seaweed for the distillery. It is a site of local humor and agricultural memory.
nature: A lush, green patch of meadowland in a largely rocky townland.

name: Poll na bPГѓВѓГ‚В©ist
description: The Worm Hole, a natural rectangular sea-pool.
folklore: Folklore speaks of a "pГѓВѓГ‚В©ist" (serpent) inhabiting the depths. It is a marvel of coastal erosion, often mistaken for a man-made quarry.
nature: A geometric basin cut into the sea-shelf by the relentless hydraulic action of the Atlantic.

name: Creig an BhobailГѓВѓГ‚В­n
description: The Rock of the Little Tassel.
folklore: Used as a marker for grazing rights and land divisions within the townland.
nature: A distinct, upright limestone block standing out from the pavement.

name: Leac na nГѓВѓГ‚В‰an
description: The Flagstone of the Birds.
folklore: A ledge where seabirds congregate in thousands during the breeding season.
nature: Exposed limestone shelf rich in maritime birdlife and salt-tolerant lichens.

name: An Aill MhГѓВѓГ‚Віr
description: The Great Cliff.
folklore: The most dramatic precipice on the island's Atlantic side, used for "clifftop vigils" by those watching for returning fishing boats.
nature: A massive vertical drop providing a cross-section of the island's limestone strata.

name: Poll na gCaorach
description: The Hole of the Sheep.
folklore: Traditionally a site where sheep were washed in sea-water or where livestock were lost during storm surges.
nature: A deep coastal inlet or blow-hole.

name: An ChurГѓВѓГ‚ВЎit
description: A small quay or landing place.
folklore: Essential for launching currachs and landing kelp; it represents the island's historical maritime economy.
nature: A sheltered cove with a man-made slipway.

name: MГѓВѓГ‚ВіinГѓВѓГ‚В­n Dubh
description: The Little Black Bog.
folklore: A rare peaty depression in the limestone.
nature: Supports moisture-loving flora like the "wood rush" and specialized mosses.

name: Port na Cille
description: The Port of the Church.
folklore: The traditional landing place for those visiting the Seven Daughters' monastic site.
nature: A natural harbor with clear access to the northern coastline.

name: An CorrГѓВѓГ‚В n
description: The Sickle, a curved coastal feature.
folklore: Descriptive of the hook-shaped rocky spit that protects the inner strand.
nature: A curved limestone ridge extending into the sea.

name: Scalp an TГѓВѓГ‚В iseir
description: Cleft of the Phantom.
folklore: Known in oral tradition as a haunted location where spirits of those lost at sea might appear.
nature: A deep, narrow fissure in the clifftop limestone.

name: Leic an NГѓВѓГ‚В­ochГѓВѓГ‚ВЎin
description: The Flagstone of the Washing.
folklore: Where island women would traditionally rinse clothing or wool in the rock-pools after rainfall.
nature: Flat, broad limestone slabs near the village.

name: Poll an RГѓВѓГ‚ВіidГѓВѓГ‚В­n
description: The Hole of the Little Path.
folklore: A coastal feature marking the end of a traditional boreen.
nature: A sea-pool accessible by a narrow rock-cut path.

name: An MionnГѓВѓГ‚ВЎn
description: A prominent coastal headland.
folklore: A critical navigational marker for fishermen returning from the Clare coast.
nature: A rocky point subject to high Atlantic swell.

name: TrГѓВѓГ‚ВЎcht Each
description: The Strand of the Horses.
folklore: Where horses were historically swum or washed in the salt water for health.
nature: A sandy coastal stretch on the northern shore.

name: ГѓВѓГ‚ВЃit an Chuain
description: The Place of the Haven.
folklore: A sheltered location within the larger harbor area.
nature: A quiet pocket of water protected from the prevailing winds.

name: Scalp ГѓВѓГ‚ВЃit an Chuain
description: The cleft associated with the haven.
folklore: Used by fishermen to store equipment or shelter from sudden storms.
nature: A geological fissure near the primary landing site.

name: An Charraig ChГѓВѓГ‚Вєl
description: The Back Rock.
folklore: A boundary marker in the maritime territory of the island.
nature: An offshore limestone outcrop visible at low tide.

name: Poll MhГѓВѓГ‚ВЎirtГѓВѓГ‚В­n
description: MartinГѓВўГ‚ВЂГ‚В™s Hole.
folklore: Named after a local individual who either found a record catch here or suffered a maritime mishap.
nature: A specific sea-cauldron within the rocky shelf.

name: Carraig an PhГѓВѓГ‚В­obaire
description: The PiperГѓВўГ‚ВЂГ‚В™s Rock.
folklore: The companion site to An Uamhain; people claimed to hear the lost piperГѓВўГ‚ВЂГ‚В™s music beneath this stone.
nature: A large, distinct limestone erratic.

name: An ChuinnГѓВѓГ‚В©og
description: The Churn, a churning sea-hole.
folklore: Named for the way the tide foams within the cavity, resembling a butter churn.
nature: A violent blow-hole that erupts during westerly gales.

name: An Poll BГѓВѓГ‚ВЎite
description: The Drowned Hole.
folklore: A sea-cave or sinkhole that remains submerged even at low tide.
nature: A deep solutional cavity on the lower sea-shelf.

name: Leac Dhubh
description: The Black Flagstone.
folklore: Notable for its dark lichen covering, distinguishing it from the surrounding grey limestone.
nature: A flat expanse of pavement near the southern cliffs.

name: Ceann na FaolchanaГѓВѓГ‚В­
description: A rocky headland on the northern coast.
folklore: A geographic marker used for dividing the island's coastal grazing.
nature: A prominent point of land jutting into the North Sound.

name: Scalp Bhrady
description: BradyГѓВўГ‚ВЂГ‚В™s Cleft.
folklore: Named for a family associated with this specific portion of the "backwaters."
nature: A narrow, vertical fissure in the coastal rock-face.

name: UlГѓВѓГ‚ВЎn Dubh
description: The Black Hillock.
folklore: A dark-colored mound, possibly of archaeological significance.
nature: A natural or man-made elevation in the limestone terrain.

name: Cois Caoi
description: Beside the Path.
folklore: A point where the main island road meets a significant historical trail.
nature: A junction in the network of limestone boreens.

name: BullГѓВѓГ‚ВЎn an Chapaill
description: The HorseГѓВўГ‚ВЂГ‚В™s Rock-Basin.
folklore: A natural solution hollow used as a trough for livestock.
nature: A basin formed by the dissolution of limestone by rainwater.

name: BГѓВѓГ‚В©al an Chalaidh
description: The Mouth of the Landing Place.
folklore: The entrance to the island's primary harbor.
nature: A deep-water channel leading to the pier.

name: Leic an ChaisleГѓВѓГ‚ВЎin
description: The Flagstone of the Castle.
folklore: Flat stones near the base of the O'Brien tower house where community meetings were held.
nature: Broad, flat limestone steps leading up to the fortress.

name: DГѓВѓГ‚Вєn na NГѓВѓГ‚В­
description: A small, ancient stone fort.
folklore: Though less famous than DГѓВѓГ‚Вєn Formna, it protected the lower coastal access points.
nature: A ruinous circular stone enclosure.

name: TrГѓВѓГ‚ВЎcht Mil
description: The Strand of Honey.
folklore: Named for its exceptionally fine, golden sand, which was once exported in small quantities.
nature: A pristine sandy beach on the southern shore.

name: Leic MhГѓВѓГ‚Віr
description: The Great Flagstone.
folklore: A massive, monolithic slab of limestone.
nature: One of the largest single pavement blocks on the island.

name: Gob an ChorrГѓВѓГ‚ВЎin
description: The Point of the Sickle.
folklore: The sharp, southern tip of the curved spit.
nature: A rocky promontory facing the open Atlantic.

name: Fordoras
description: The Lintle or "Outer Door."
folklore: A natural stone passage that acts as a gateway to the lighthouse area.
nature: A narrow gap between high limestone walls.

name: Loig BhuГѓВѓГ‚В­
description: The Yellow Hollow.
folklore: Named for the vibrant growth of gorse and yellow lichens.
nature: A sheltered depression providing a micro-habitat for diverse flora.

name: Poll Loige BuГѓВѓГ‚В­
description: The hole within the yellow hollow.
folklore: A vertical sinkhole or "swallow-hole" that drains the hollow during heavy rain.
nature: A deep karst pipe extending into the limestone.

name: An ScreigГѓВѓГ‚В­n
description: The Little Stony Place.
folklore: An area of loose, weathered limestone rubble (scree).
nature: A field of broken rock fragments, difficult to traverse.

name: Cloch Chormaic
description: CormacГѓВўГ‚ВЂГ‚В™s Stone.
folklore: A memorial or boundary marker named for a historical islander.
nature: A large, isolated limestone boulder.

name: Poll DhГѓВѓГ‚Віnaill
description: DonaldГѓВўГ‚ВЂГ‚В™s Hole.
folklore: Associated with a specific maritime accident or a legendary fisherman named Donald.
nature: A deep, dangerous coastal inlet.

name: Caladh ThrГѓВѓГ‚ВЎ Caorach
description: The Landing Place of Sheep Strand.
folklore: Where livestock were traditionally boated to the mainland.
nature: A sheltered beach access with calm water.

name: TrГѓВѓГ‚ВЎ Caorach
description: Sheep Strand.
folklore: Used for washing sheep and as a collection point for kelp.
nature: A broad sandy beach on the eastern side.

name: Gob na Cora
description: The Point of the Weir.
folklore: Site of an ancient stone fish weir, visible at very low tides.
nature: A coastal point with man-made stone remains.

name: Poll an RГѓВѓГ‚Віiste
description: The Hole of the Roach.
folklore: Named for the abundance of specific rock-fish found here.
nature: A deep, calm sea-pool within the rocky shelf.

name: Carraig na Finnise
description: The reef where the Plassy was wrecked.
folklore: Known to mariners as one of the most treacherous spots in the South Sound.
nature: A submerged limestone ridge that breaks the surface only at low tide.

name: An PhoillГѓВѓГ‚В­n
description: The Little Pool.
folklore: A seasonal wetland that attracts migratory wading birds.
nature: A shallow depression in the karst that retains water.

name: CreagГѓВѓГ‚ВЎn Ard
description: The High Little Rock.
folklore: An elevated point of the limestone pavement offering views of the Clare coast.
nature: A prominent karst outcrop.

name: An Loch MГѓВѓГ‚Віr
description: The Big Lake.
folklore: The island's largest body of water, a brackish lagoon.
nature: Supports a unique ecosystem of eels and saltwater-tolerant plants.

name: Droim
description: The Ridge.
folklore: A long, low limestone elevation running through the center of the island.
nature: A fundamental geological feature of Inis OГѓВѓГ‚В­rr.

name: Leac Chrannait
description: A holy site associated with St. Crannat.
folklore: Pilgrims visit this flagstone to offer prayers for sight and clarity.
nature: A flat ritual stone near a historical well.

name: Creig na Fola
description: The Rock of the Blood.
folklore: Local lore suggests this was the site of a massacre during the Cromwellian invasion.
nature: A dark, stained-looking limestone terrace.

name: Aill Chreig an TГѓВѓГ‚В­
description: The Cliff of the House-Rock.
folklore: Named for a house that once stood perilously close to the cliff edge.
nature: A vertical coastal section near Baile an ChaisleГѓВѓГ‚ВЎin.

name: CeathrГѓВѓГ‚Вє I
description: The First Quarter.
folklore: One of the four primary land divisions of Inis OГѓВѓГ‚В­rr.
nature: Administrative and agricultural territory.

name: CeathrГѓВѓГ‚Вє II
description: The Second Quarter.
folklore: Historically the most fertile division of the island.
nature: A mix of sandy soil and limestone terraces.

name: CeathrГѓВѓГ‚Вє III
description: The Third Quarter.
folklore: Contains many of the islandГѓВўГ‚ВЂГ‚В™s primary archaeological sites.
nature: Rich in ancient stone walls and ruins.

name: An Gleann
description: The Valley.
folklore: A rare sheltered depression between the villages and the Formna ridge.
nature: Provides protection for the island's few trees (mainly sycamores).

name: Gleann an Fhormna
description: The valley of the ridge.
folklore: A deep-map transition point between the villages and the high forts.
nature: A geological fault-line in the limestone.

name: An BhlГѓВѓГ‚Вєibh
description: A small, sheltered hollow in Baile an tSГѓВѓГ‚В©ipГѓВѓГ‚В©il.
folklore: Often used for sheltering livestock during heavy winter gales.
nature: A natural limestone basin.

name: Leic na nGabhar
description: The Flagstone of the Goats.
folklore: Where wild or semi-feral goats congregate on the higher crags.
nature: An exposed, high-altitude limestone terrace.

name: An Chreig MhГѓВѓГ‚Віr
description: The Great Crag.
folklore: A massive expanse of karst pavement mentioned as a hiding place during the 1920 raids.
nature: Extensive limestone terrace with deep grykes.

name: Port na hAille
description: The Port of the Cliff.
folklore: A narrow landing point used only in perfect sea conditions.
nature: A rocky cleft providing access to the deep Atlantic water.

name: An TrГѓВѓГ‚ВЎ
description: The Strand.
folklore: The primary beach of Inis OГѓВѓГ‚В­rr, central to social and economic life.
nature: A large sandy beach protected by the harbor pier.

name: Hill Farm
description: Site where three Galway Volunteers hid in 1920.
folklore: The islanders provided refuge for the men in a store here, avoiding a potential gun battle with the Black and Tans.
nature: A farm complex on the slopes leading to the ridge.

name: Johnston's Big Fields
description: Large meadows in front of Kilmurvey House (Mainistir area).
folklore: Used by the Volunteers for drilling in secret; Johnston himself was a supporter of the Crown, adding to the local tension.
nature: Significant area of reclaimed pastureland.

name: Atlantic Hotel
description: The primary hostelry for visiting writers and scholars.
folklore: Visited by Synge and Lady Gregory in 1898; it was the "embassy" for the Irish literary revival on the islands.
nature: A stone building that served as the cultural hub of the island for decades.

name: An BГѓВѓГ‚ВіithrГѓВѓГ‚В­n
description: The Little Road.
folklore: A traditional narrow path through the limestone fields.
nature: A hallmark of the Aran cultural landscape.

name: Pier of Inis OГѓВѓГ‚В­rr
description: The primary point of contact with the mainland.
folklore: Associated with the arrival of the Duras and Dun Aengus steamers in the early 20th century.
nature: A heavy stone structure resistant to the North Sound's currents.

name: Formna Height
description: The peak of the island's central ridge.
folklore: Offers a 360-degree view of the archipelago and the mainland.
nature: High-point of the limestone karst.

name: The Grotto
description: A religious shrine located in a cliff nook.
folklore: A focus for community prayer, especially during the Marian Year.
nature: A small cave-like opening decorated with religious icons.

name: CaisleГѓВѓГ‚ВЎn Area
description: The territory surrounding the O'Brien castle.
folklore: The historical center of political power on Inis OГѓВѓГ‚В­rr.
nature: Strategically located high ground.

name: The Old School
description: Ruins of the National School near the signal tower.
folklore: Represents the 19th-century educational efforts by the British government.
nature: Stone ruins on the exposed northern coast.

name: Signal Tower
description: A late 18th-century defensive structure.
folklore: Part of a chain of towers built to watch for a French invasion during the Napoleonic Wars.
nature: A square stone tower on a prominent coastal height.

name: Baile an DГѓВѓГ‚Вєin
description: The village surrounding the prehistoric fort.
folklore: One of the oldest continuously inhabited clusters on the island.
nature: Densely packed stone cottages near the ridge.

name: An Lurgain
description: The Long Slope.
folklore: A significant geographical feature that defines the islandГѓВўГ‚ВЂГ‚В™s northern aspect.
nature: A long, steady limestone incline.

name: Poll an RГѓВѓГ‚ВіidГѓВѓГ‚В­n
description: Coastal sea-pool near the "little path."
folklore: Named for its accessibility via a secret or minor boreen.
nature: A sheltered coastal nook.

name: DГѓВѓГ‚Вєn Eochla
description: A massive, multi-walled stone fort situated at the highest point of Inishmore in the Eochaill townland.
folklore: Robinson notes it as a primary landmark for antiquarians. George Petrie visited in 1821 to document its "Military Architecture," providing the first scientific discipline to Irish archaeology.
nature: Positioned on a high limestone plateau with 360-degree visibility across the islandГѓВўГ‚ВЂГ‚В™s terraced "desert."

images.author: Roger Diel
images.source: https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Dun Eochla Stone fort on Inishmore, the Aran Islands - geograph.org.uk - 2342166.jpg

name: Na Seacht dTeampaill (Seven Churches)
description: A monastic complex in Eoghanacht, consisting of ruined churches and domestic structures.
folklore: Dedicated to St. Brecan. Despite the name, Robinson clarifies it consists of only two churches (Teampall BhreacГѓВѓГ‚ВЎin and Teampall an Phoill) and several peripheral ruins. It was a major pilgrimage site where stones were once "miraculous."
nature: Surrounded by limestone pavements and small fertile patches near the northern Atlantic coast.

images.author: P L Chadwick
images.source: https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Minibus approaching Na Seacht dTeampaill (Seven Churches), near Onaght, InishmГѓВѓГ‚Віr (ГѓВѓГ‚ВЃrainn), Aran Islands, Co. Galway - geograph.org.uk - 3634989.jpg

images.author: P L Chadwick
images.source: https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Ford minibus by Na Seacht dTeampaill (Seven Churches), near Onaght, InishmГѓВѓГ‚Віr (ГѓВѓГ‚ВЃrainn), Aran Islands, Co. Galway - geograph.org.uk - 3635030.jpg

images.author: P L Chadwick
images.source: https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Mercedes minibus by Na Seacht dTeampaill (Seven Churches), near Onaght, InishmГѓВѓГ‚Віr (ГѓВѓГ‚ВЃrainn), Aran Islands, Co. Galway - geograph.org.uk - 3635007.jpg

name: DГѓВѓГ‚Вєn Eoghanachta
description: A perfectly circular stone fort located near the village of Eoghanacht.
folklore: Named after the Eoghanachta clan of Munster. Robinson reflects on its culmination of the 1864 expedition where puffins "forsook their native cliffs in sheer fright" due to the cheers for the Queen.
nature: Sits on a level limestone terrace; the base is surrounded by broken limestone fragments and specialized flora.

images.author: Jonathan Wilkins
images.source: https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Ringfort de DГѓВѓГ‚Вєn Eoghanachta, ГѓВѓГ‚В®le dГѓВўГ‚ВЂГ‚В™ Inishmore.jpg

name: Cill Muirbhigh Barracks
description: A former Royal Irish Constabulary outpost in Cill Muirbhigh.
folklore: Closed in 1887. Robinson details its closure as a sign of the "condition of the west" improving, following decades of agitating for better school buildings by Fr. O'Donoghue.
nature: Located near the sheltered sandy bay of Cill Muirbhigh, away from the direct Atlantic gales.

name: Tobar BhreacГѓВѓГ‚ВЎin (St. Brecan's Well)
description: A holy well within the Na Seacht dTeampaill complex.
folklore: Dedicated to the founder of the monastery; traditional prayer patterns involved healing rituals for pilgrims.
nature: A natural spring issuing from a limestone fissure, often hosting moisture-loving mosses.

name: Leaba RГѓВѓГ‚ВіnГѓВѓГ‚ВЎin (RГѓВѓГ‚ВіnГѓВѓГ‚ВЎnГѓВўГ‚ВЂГ‚В™s Bed)
description: A stone enclosure or "bed" in Mainistir, believed to be the saint's grave.
folklore: Historically, locals slept in the "leaba" on the eve of the saint's day (August 15th). In 1947, Fr. Killeen cleared the site for a procession, felling an "alder tree" (the last saintly tree), which caused deep local resentment.
nature: A square plot of four yards square, delimited by low walls, featuring a stone inscribed with a cross.

name: Scailp ГѓВѓГ‚ВЃlainn
description: A notable fissure or "beautiful recess" along the northern cliffs.
folklore: Described as a place of refuge. Robinson uses it to highlight the aesthetic wonder that early 19th-century "sentimental tourists" sought in the sublime landscape.
nature: A deep cleft in the limestone, home to the "sweetly mottled" maiden-hair fern (Adiantum capillus-veneris).

name: An Aill BhrГѓВѓГ‚В­ste (The Broken Cliff)
description: A section of cliff-face in Mainistir that steepens into a vertical scarp.
folklore: Site of a "grotto" containing a blue and white Madonna. Robinson ironically notes a "minute goldcrest"ГѓВўГ‚ВЂГ‚В”a rare bird in AranГѓВўГ‚ВЂГ‚В”flitting in the tapestry of rambling roses near the niche.
nature: Features "shadowy greenery" and a vertical rock-face transitioning into a rugged limestone scarp.

name: Log na Marbh (Hollow of the Dead)
description: A burial mound located below the road in Mainistir, near a modern water tank.
folklore: Site of a 1584 clan battle between the descendants of Owen O'Flaherty and Murchadh na dTua. Tradition holds nine murdered Connemara men lie here in a "backwater of Aran history."
nature: A squarish mound featuring traces of stone kerbing and a small upright stone marker amidst wild flowers.

name: Digby House
description: The ruins of a three-storey dwelling in Mainistir, near the leaba.
folklore: Built for the Digbys, the islandsГѓВўГ‚ВЂГ‚В™ absentee landlords. By the 1840s, it was in ruins; its stones were later repurposed for SyngeГѓВўГ‚ВЂГ‚В™s rectory and the Catholic curate's house.
nature: Overlooks the lower pasturage and the site associated with the "Hollow of the Dead."

name: An SuicГѓВѓГ‚В­n (The Little Jobber)
description: A derelict area behind Joe Watty's pub, formerly the "poorest quarter" of the village.
folklore: Derived from "something that sucks" (a marshy patch). Robinson identifies it as a place where the menfolk lounged at "CoirnГѓВѓГ‚В©al na mBrГѓВѓГ‚В©ag" (the corner of lies).
nature: A damp, low-lying area with the decayed ends of thatched cottage rows still visible.

name: An Jabaire Beag (Jobber's Hill)
description: A rise on the road out of Cill RГѓВѓГ‚ВіnГѓВѓГ‚ВЎin, named after Pat MullenГѓВўГ‚ВЂГ‚В™s uncle.
folklore: Named after a cattle-dealer (jobber) whose cottage was a meeting-place for "the man from the east and the man from the west." Famous for stickfights and faction brawls.
nature: A limestone rise (carcair) offering views back toward the harbor and the Technical School.

name: The Residence
description: A standard-design building from the 1880s nestled under a central peak.
folklore: The birthplace of writer BreandГѓВѓГ‚ВЎn ГѓВѓГ‚В“ hEithir in 1930. His father was a teacher from Co. Clare who married Liam O'Flaherty's sister from Gort na gCapall.
nature: A sturdy stone structure typical of the Congested Districts Board (CDB) era of development.

name: Joe WattyГѓВўГ‚ВЂГ‚В™s Pub
description: A public house on the edge of Cill RГѓВѓГ‚ВіnГѓВѓГ‚ВЎin, formerly obscure.
folklore: Joe Watty himself died in 1975 (the same day as de Valera). He was known for standing in prayer for twenty minutes every day at the "cair" opposite the pub.
nature: Located at a junction where the level coast road meets the steep hills of the interior.

name: The "Cair"
description: A small stone seat or structure opposite Joe WattyГѓВўГ‚ВЂГ‚В™s Pub.
folklore: Used by Joe Watty as a site of daily contemplation before the arrival of the mail steamer.
nature: A simple limestone feature integrated into the dry-stone field walls.

name: Hill Farm
description: A farmstead in Fearann an Choirce with a prominent store.
folklore: In December 1920, three Galway Volunteers (Thomas Fleming, Thomas O'Flaherty) hid in a store here. They were nearly betrayed but escaped before Black and Tan forces arrived.
nature: Surrounded by the "big fields" cleared of stone, representing the island's best agricultural land.

name: The Fish Market
description: A designated area at the Kilronan pier/quay.
folklore: Site of the 1852 riot where Rev. Hamilton Synge was mobbed. Synge presented a loaded musket and threatened to shoot his pursuers before the police intervened.
nature: A bustling area of the harbor constructed of massive hand-hewn limestone blocks.

name: Digby Eviction Site
description: Locations in Cill RГѓВѓГ‚ВіnГѓВѓГ‚ВЎin and Mainistir associated with the Land League.
folklore: David O'Callaghan was a central figure in the inaugural 1880 Land League meeting, presided over by PГѓВѓГ‚ВЎdraic Pearse. Conflict centered on the "rent-books" stolen from the courthouse.
nature: Sites marked by the interface of village dwellings and the surrounding "stony" fields.

name: An Chreig Dhubh (The Black Crag)
description: A vast terrace of limestone pavement in Eochaill.
folklore: Islanders like "Old Beartla" considered it a disgrace that this wasteland was never reclaimed. Robinson describes it as a "lugubrious" and daunting "desert."
nature: A high, flat expanse of bare limestone with deep fissures (grikes) and extreme exposure.

name: Buaile na gCopГѓВѓГ‚Віg (Pasture of Plantains)
description: A wedge of ground between the road and the scarp in Eochaill.
folklore: A micro-toponym denoting a place once used for milking or summer pasturage, specifically rich in plantains.
nature: A rare patch of greenery amidst the crags, containing hardy herbs and small shrubs.

name: Carcair na gCeГѓВѓГ‚ВЎrtan (Slope of the Forge)
description: A steep ascent on the road toward Eochaill.
folklore: Named after a forge run by Micil Riabhach ГѓВѓГ‚В“ Niaidh, a Connemara poitin smuggler of a century ago.
nature: A sharp limestone incline where the road cuts through the geological ridge.

name: An lui mГѓВѓГ‚Віr (The Big Lie-down)
description: A specific field name on BeartlaГѓВўГ‚ВЂГ‚В™s farm.
folklore: Named for a horse that spent all day lying down there after a grueling morning carting seaweed.
nature: A patch of icy-looking limestone pavement with sparse grass.

name: Lawrence Mc Donogh Memorial
description: A small stone plaque set among the stones of a field-wall in Mainistir.
folklore: Marks the spot where Mc Donogh was shot by "Crown Forces" (Black and Tans) on December 19, 1920.
nature: Integrated into a traditional dry-stone wall along the quiet "backwater" road.

name: The "Gap in the Wall"
description: A specific breach in a field wall near the Cill RГѓВѓГ‚ВіnГѓВѓГ‚ВЎin coast road.
folklore: Site where Larry Beag was shot by a Black and Tan sniper with a telescopic sight while trying to sneak to Mass in 1920.
nature: A typical agricultural gap, now serving as a somber historical marker of the War of Independence.

name: BГѓВѓГ‚ВіithrГѓВѓГ‚В­n na gCottageachaГѓВѓГ‚В­
description: A road built by the Congested Districts Board in 1915.
folklore: Constructed to replace "hovels" with semi-detached cottages. Robinson notes the name as a linguistic hybrid he calls "CDB-Irish."
nature: A straight, modern-style road contrasting with the ancient, winding boreens.

name: Minister's Gate
description: A location in Cill RГѓВѓГ‚ВіnГѓВѓГ‚ВЎin associated with the 1920 raids.
folklore: MГѓВѓГ‚ВЎire GillГѓВўГ‚ВЂГ‚В™s two uncles were seized here by Black and Tans and dragged behind the gate while their grandmother was brought out to see them shot.
nature: A formal entranceway near the old rectory and Protestant church grounds.

name: Creig MhГѓВѓГ‚Віr (The Great Crag)
description: A massive limestone terrace used as a refuge.
folklore: PГѓВѓГ‚ВЎidraic ГѓВѓГ‚В“ hIarnГѓВѓГ‚ВЎin hid in a specific crevice here with his gun to escape the Black and Tans on a "dim winter dawn" in 1920.
nature: High-elevation limestone pavement featuring deep, protective "grikes" and crevices.

name: Gort na gCapall
description: A field near the O'Flaherty house in the townland of the same name.
folklore: Site where Black and Tans drilled on the road and in "Johnston's big fields," to the annoyance of the local inhabitants.
nature: Large, level fields representing some of the more fertile limestone-soil mixtures.

name: Creig Earraigh (Aristo's Crag)
description: A crag in Eochaill associated with local oral tradition.
folklore: Local folklore links the name to the philosopher Aristotle ("Aristo"), depicted as a "very wise, very brainy" figure who outwitted a housekeeper to marry her.
nature: A rugged limestone terrace; the name is a corruption of a more ancient topographic term.

name: Harry StattleГѓВўГ‚ВЂГ‚В™s Crag
description: A crag in Eochaill with a cryptic local name.
folklore: Associated with the saying "SГѓВѓГ‚ВЎraigh sГѓВѓГ‚В© Harai Steatail" (He beats Harry Stattle), though the identity of Harry remains a mystery to current islanders.
nature: A characteristically stony limestone pavement with deep fissures.

name: The Protestant School
description: A former educational building in Cill RГѓВѓГ‚ВіnГѓВѓГ‚ВЎin funded in 1826.
folklore: Funded by the London Hibernian Society; it was a site of intense sectarian tension between the "Jumpers" and the Catholic majority.
nature: A small, sturdy building constructed of local limestone blocks.

name: ChardeГѓВўГ‚ВЂГ‚В™s Shop
description: A former grocery and bakery in Mainistir.
folklore: Run by Thomas Charde; it was boycotted in 1868 during a sectarian dispute over four "kidnapped" Catholic children raised as Protestants.
nature: Located in the heart of the Mainistir hamlet, near the junction.

name: GanlyГѓВўГ‚ВЂГ‚В™s Farm
description: A farmstead in Mainistir, site of land conflict.
folklore: Pat Ganly, son of the pier-builder, fought a "vicious" legal and physical battle against Rev. Kilbride over land ownership in the 1870s.
nature: Features several large fields that were the focus of the "land-grabbing" accusations.

name: The "Jumper's Boat"
description: A vessel named the Georgiana (Rev. Synge's yacht).
folklore: Used to transport Protestant supplies; it was an object of such resentment that Claddagh fishermen attacked it with "sunfish spears" in 1853.
nature: Usually moored in the Cill RГѓВѓГ‚ВіnГѓВѓГ‚ВЎin harbor, visible to all "hostile" villagers.

name: Arkin Castle Site
description: Ruins of a Cromwellian fortification on an O'Flaherty castle site.
folklore: Represented the "long shadow" of Cromwell. William Wilde documented its ruins in 1857 as a symbol of the "Saxon" innovation.
nature: Located at the edge of Cill RГѓВѓГ‚ВіnГѓВѓГ‚ВЎin where the built village meets the sea.

name: Carcair an Atharla
description: The slope leading down to the burial ground in Mainistir.
folklore: Atharla refers to an ancient church site. It is the path where funeral processions descended toward St. Ronan's bed.
nature: A gentle limestone slope leading toward the coast, lined with small barns.

name: Log na gCorp
description: A specific hollow in Mainistir.
folklore: Historically used as a resting place for coffins during the long walk to the cemetery.
nature: A sheltered depression protected from the wind by high stone walls.

name: Teampall Macduach
description: A medieval church ruin in Cill Muirbhigh.
folklore: Features high-quality masonry; Robinson notes it was built by a local stonecutter, John Burke, in the last century.
nature: Sits on a limestone terrace with views of the northern sound.

name: The "Old Lighthouse"
description: Disused buildings on the Eochaill heights.
folklore: In 1880, islanders were fined for taking stones from these buildings to repair field walls, an act of "public lies" against the agent Thompson.
nature: High-elevation site with extreme exposure; the buildings were stripped of their utility by the late 19th century.

images.author: Colin Park
images.source: https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:On Inishmore - The old lighthouse near Dun Eochla - geograph.org.uk - 6396252.jpg

name: Carcair na bPГѓВѓГ‚ВєcaГѓВѓГ‚В­
description: A slope associated with the "Pooka" or spirits.
folklore: Legend warns of supernatural encounters here; it is one of the "short-cuts" linking Baile an DГѓВѓГ‚Вєin with the main road.
nature: A winding section of the road with high walls that create deep shadows at night.

name: RГѓВѓГ‚ВіidГѓВѓГ‚В­n an PhГѓВѓГ‚Вєca
description: A small road or "little road of the pooka."
folklore: Described by Robinson as "ancient, twisty and haunted," leading toward the hamlet of Baile an DГѓВѓГ‚Вєin.
nature: Narrow and bordering ancient dry-stone walls.

name: An GleannachГѓВѓГ‚ВЎn
description: A small valley or depression near Eoghanacht.
folklore: Site of botanical interest during the 1864 expedition; Robinson notes it as a rare "lush" spot.
nature: Features a unique microclimate supporting more vegetation than the open crags.

name: Portmurvey (Port Muirbhigh)
description: A coastal inlet and pier.
folklore: In 1857, excursionists including William Wilde and Samuel Ferguson landed here. Robinson notes the abundance of the "handsome rose-beetle" (Cetonia aurata).
nature: A sandy-bottomed bay with low-lying dunes and "sweetly mottled" ferns in the rock chinks.

name: The Imperial Hotel
description: A lodging house in Cill RГѓВѓГ‚ВіnГѓВѓГ‚ВЎin.
folklore: Associated with visiting antiquarians like Artaud, who left his bill unpaid before being arrested in Dublin.
nature: A prominent stone building overlooking the Cill RГѓВѓГ‚ВіnГѓВѓГ‚ВЎin harbor.

name: The Atlantic Hotel
description: A hotel in Cill RГѓВѓГ‚ВіnГѓВѓГ‚ВЎin.
folklore: Site where early Celticists like Heinrich Zimmer and the "statistic" tourists of the 19th century stayed.
nature: Located on the main street of Cill RГѓВѓГ‚ВіnГѓВѓГ‚ВЎin village.

name: An Port Garbh
description: A "Rough Port" on the northern shore.
folklore: Known for treacherous currents. Robinson notes the "broken cliffs" here as a site of extreme geological activity.
