nature: A jagged coastline with massive limestone blocks scattered by Atlantic storms.

name: The "DoctorГѓВўГ‚ВЂГ‚В™s Grave"
description: An unmarked grave in the Protestant churchyard.
folklore: The resting place of Dr. Stoney, who died of "black infection" in 1869 after a life of laudanum addiction and "black tragi-comedies."
nature: Located in the farthest corner of the churchyard, marked only by two small boulders.

name: An BГѓВѓГ‚Віthar BuГѓВѓГ‚В­
description: The "Yellow Road" leading out of Cill RГѓВѓГ‚ВіnГѓВѓГ‚ВЎin.
folklore: Named for the color of the local clay used to level the limestone surface.
nature: A dusty, bright trackway cutting through the grey limestone landscape.

name: Gort na gCeГѓВѓГ‚ВЎrta
description: Field of the Forges.
folklore: Traditionally associated with metalworkers; Robinson notes the persistence of such names despite the disappearance of the trade.
nature: A rectangular field containing traces of ancient stone-working.

name: An Lorg
description: The Track or ancient path in SruthГѓВѓГ‚ВЎn.
folklore: A worn trackway etched into the bare limestone pavement, used for centuries by locals to navigate the crags.
nature: A distinct linear feature in the otherwise uniform limestone "desert."

name: An Tuar
description: The "Bleaching Green."
folklore: A flat limestone terrace once used for drying linen or wool in the sun.
nature: A level, clean expanse of rock with high solar exposure.

name: The "Signal Tower" Site
description: Location of a Napoleonic-era signal station on the heights.
folklore: Part of the 18th-century coastal defense network; Robinson reflects on its strategic but desolate position.
nature: High-elevation site with 360-degree views of the Atlantic and the Galway coast.

name: An Carraig Fhada
description: The Long Rock reef.
folklore: A major navigational hazard for the "Jumper's Boat" and other vessels entering the harbor.
nature: A long, dark limestone feature visible only at low tide.

name: Buaile na nUan
description: Pasture of the Lambs.
folklore: A small, sheltered enclosure used specifically for protecting young sheep in the spring.
nature: A tiny walled field with a rare patch of nutritious grass.

name: An Carraig Rua
description: The Red Rock.
folklore: Named for the reddish lichen that covers its surface, a biological marker of the clean island air.
nature: A distinct limestone outcrop near the Eochaill townland border.

name: Gort na hEille
description: Field of the Thong.
folklore: Associated with a legend of a land measurement using a leather thong to outwit a landlord.
nature: A long, narrow field following a natural geological fault.

name: An Screig Rua
description: The Red Crag.
folklore: Features unique iron-rich staining in the limestone, giving it a distinct hue in the rain.
nature: Red-tinted limestone pavement with deep, dark grikes.

name: An Buaile MГѓВѓГ‚Віr
description: The "Large Pasture" of SruthГѓВѓГ‚ВЎn.
folklore: The primary grazing area for the villageГѓВўГ‚ВЂГ‚В™s livestock, representing communal agricultural effort.
nature: A vast, walled enclosure with some natural soil accumulation.

name: An SuicГѓВѓГ‚В­n Thiar
description: The "Western SuicГѓВѓГ‚В­n."
folklore: A continuation of the marshy patch where water naturally collects from the Eochaill heights.
nature: A damp hollow filled with rushes and water-loving vegetation.

name: An Buaile Beag
description: The Small Pasture.
folklore: A miniature enclosure used for individual animals or "hospital" grazing.
nature: A tiny walled field, often the result of centuries of stone-clearing.

name: An Port BuГѓВѓГ‚В­
description: The Yellow Port.
folklore: A small cove with yellow sand, a rarity on the northern "broken" limestone coast.
nature: A small, sandy inlet sheltered by low limestone cliffs.

name: The "PriestГѓВўГ‚ВЂГ‚В™s Wall"
description: A high wall in Cill RГѓВѓГ‚ВіnГѓВѓГ‚ВЎin.
folklore: Historically associated with the Catholic clergy's efforts to define and protect their domain from the "Jumpers."
nature: A high, well-maintained stone wall that blocks the Atlantic wind.

name: An Carraig Dubh
description: The Black Rock.
folklore: An outcrop that remains wet and dark even in dry weather due to moss coverage.
nature: Covered in dark mosses and specialized lichens.

name: Gort an DГѓВѓГ‚ВєidГѓВѓГ‚В­n
description: Field of the Pipe.
folklore: Named after a legendary smoker or a found clay pipe in the soil.
nature: A small, rectangular field with high dry-stone walls.

name: The "Eviction Stone"
description: A marker near the Land League meeting site.
folklore: Commemorates the resistance of the islanders against the Digby bailiffs and the "rent-war."
nature: A natural stone integrated into the historical narrative of Cill RГѓВѓГ‚ВіnГѓВѓГ‚ВЎin.

name: An Log
description: The Hollow.
folklore: A natural amphitheater used for "whispering consultation" and secret meetings during the Land War.
nature: A deep, sheltered depression in the limestone.

name: An Gort MГѓВѓГ‚Віr
description: The Large Field in Eoghanacht.
folklore: One of the few fields large enough for communal potato planting or "meitheal."
nature: Level limestone soil with a high concentration of clover.

name: An Pholl Dubh
description: The Black Hole.
folklore: A deep swallow-hole or cave entrance. Robinson notes these as sites where water "sucks" into the island's core.
nature: Surrounded by ferns and often containing standing water after gales.

name: An CarraigГѓВѓГ‚В­n
description: The Little Rock.
folklore: A navigational landmark used by currach fishermen to identify the northern approach.
nature: A distinct limestone outcrop near the shoreline.

name: Buaile na gCapall
description: Pasture of the Horses.
folklore: Historically used to graze the O'Flaherty clan's horses.
nature: A larger enclosed field with some natural grass cover.

name: The "Alder Tree" Site
description: Location near the Mainistir burial ground.
folklore: Site where the last saintly tree was cut down in 1947, an event Robinson links to the loss of "Aran simplicity."
nature: A small enclosure once capable of supporting a single tree.

name: An Carraig MhГѓВѓГ‚Віr
description: The Big Rock.
folklore: A massive navigational hazard near the northern "broken cliffs."
nature: A massive limestone boulder partially submerged in the Atlantic.

name: Buaile na nEach
description: Pasture of the Steeds.
folklore: Associated with the high-status horses of the medieval O'Flaherty chieftains.
nature: A sheltered field with high, protective stone walls.

name: The "Steamer" Pier
description: The main landing point for the SS Duras.
folklore: Robinson describes its construction as the "transformation of the seaward aspect of Cill RГѓВѓГ‚ВіnГѓВѓГ‚ВЎin."
nature: A modern (late 19th-century) stone pier made of massive limestone.

name: An Gort Glas
description: The Green Field.
folklore: A rare and precious fertile enclosure amidst the stony townland of Fearann an Choirce.
nature: Lush green grass protected by high stone walls.

name: An Chreig MhГѓВѓГ‚Віr Refuge
description: A specific section of the crag used in 1920.
folklore: The location where a woman hid a money-box under her petticoats as Black and Tans burst into her house.
nature: Characterized by high stone walls and proximity to the village center.

name: The "Minister's House"
description: The old rectory in Cill RГѓВѓГ‚ВіnГѓВѓГ‚ВЎin.
folklore: Associated with Rev. Kilbride, who was described by locals as a "bigot" and a "parson in council."
nature: A large stone building set in its own grounds, now largely disused.

name: Log na nGadhar
description: Hollow of the Dogs.
folklore: Local lore suggests it was a site where hunting dogs were traditionally kept or trained.
nature: A sheltered depression in the limestone.

name: An SuicГѓВѓГ‚В­n Theas
description: The "Southern SuicГѓВѓГ‚В­n."
folklore: A continuation of the poor quarter where men gathered to "exchange stories."
nature: Known as the "corner of lies" (CoirnГѓВѓГ‚В©al na mBrГѓВѓГ‚В©ag).

name: The "Post Office" Hill
description: A rise in Cill RГѓВѓГ‚ВіnГѓВѓГ‚ВЎin village.
folklore: Location where islanders gathered to wait for news and mail from the SS Duras.
nature: A paved rise in the built-up area of the village.

name: An CarraigГѓВѓГ‚В­n Rua
description: The Little Red Rock.
folklore: Named for the red-tinted lichens that grow on its sheltered side.
nature: A small outcrop near the coast road.

name: BГѓВѓГ‚ВіithrГѓВѓГ‚В­n na bPГѓВѓГ‚ВЎgГѓВѓГ‚ВЎnach
description: Lane of the Pagans.
folklore: Folklore suggests this was an ancient route used before the arrival of St. Enda.
nature: A very narrow and ancient-looking lane with high, mossy walls.

name: An Screig BhГѓВѓГ‚ВЎn
description: The White Crag.
folklore: Named for its brilliant white appearance when the limestone is dry.
nature: Pure, unweathered limestone pavement with few grikes.

name: Buaile na gCluas
description: Pasture of the Ears.
folklore: A field with a cryptic name, possibly referring to a site of keen hearing or "listening" for the sea.
nature: A sheltered field near the northern coast.

name: An Carraig Dubh
description: The Black Rock (Coastal).
folklore: A navigational landmark for the northern fishing grounds.
nature: A dark, jagged rock visible at low tide.

name: Gort an tSionnaigh
description: Field of the Fox.
folklore: Robinson notes this as an ironic name since foxes are not native to the islands.
nature: A small, rocky field in the Eochaill townland.

name: An Port Beag
description: The Small Port.
folklore: A tiny inlet used for launching currachs in calm weather.
nature: A small, stony beach sheltered by limestone headlands.

name: The "CDB Cottages"
description: Semi-detached dwellings along BГѓВѓГ‚ВіithrГѓВѓГ‚В­n na gCottageachaГѓВѓГ‚В­.
folklore: Built in 1915 as part of the "Killing Home Rule by Kindness" government policy.
nature: Identical stone dwellings that represent the first "modern" housing on the island.

name: An CarraigГѓВѓГ‚В­n BГѓВѓГ‚ВЎn
description: The Little White Rock.
folklore: A landmark used for aligning fishing nets in the northern sound.
nature: A small, bright limestone feature on the shoreline.

name: Gort na gCloch
description: Field of the Stones.
folklore: A field that remains incredibly stony despite generations of clearing effort.
nature: Covered in loose limestone fragments and "broken" rock.

name: An Buaile Dubh
description: The Black Pasture.
folklore: Named for the dark, peaty soil found in this specific limestone depression.
nature: A low-lying hollow with slightly darker topsoil than the surrounding crags.

name: Carcair na gCeann
description: Slope of the Heads.
folklore: Sometimes linked to ancient battle sites (1584) where skulls were allegedly unearthed.
nature: A steep rise with many loose limestone fragments.

name: The "Grotto" Madonna
description: A statue in the cliffs of An Aill BhrГѓВѓГ‚В­ste.
folklore: A blue and white figure that Robinson views as a "Crivelli" masterpiece in the wild.
nature: Nestled among "shadowy greenery" and flitting goldcrests.

name: An ScreigГѓВѓГ‚В­n
description: The Little Crag.
folklore: Known for its absolute lack of soil; Robinson describes it as a "desert" of rock.
nature: Flat, weather-beaten limestone pavement.

name: Buaile na gCapall (Eoghanacht)
description: A horse pasture in the western townland.
folklore: Historically associated with the transport of seaweed to the internal fields.
nature: A larger enclosed field with some natural grass cover.

name: An Port Garbh (North)
description: A "Rough Port" on the northern Atlantic side.
folklore: Known for its "broken" cliffs and the extreme force of the Atlantic gales.
nature: A jagged coastline with massive limestone blocks.

name: An Chreig MhГѓВѓГ‚Віr (SruthГѓВѓГ‚ВЎn)
description: The "Great Crag" of SruthГѓВѓГ‚ВЎn.
folklore: A massive expanse of limestone that served as a communal grazing area.
nature: A high, flat terrace of limestone with deep, protective fissures.

name: An Gort Glas (Mainistir)
description: The Green Field of Mainistir.
folklore: A rare and precious fertile field near the monastery ruins.
nature: Lush green grass protected by high stone walls.

name: An SuicГѓВѓГ‚В­n (Cill RГѓВѓГ‚ВіnГѓВѓГ‚ВЎin)
description: The "marshy patch" south of the pub.
folklore: Robinson identifies it as the "poorest quarter" with terraces of thatched cottages.
nature: A damp, low-lying area where water collects.

name: An Buaile BГѓВѓГ‚ВЎn
description: The White Pasture.
folklore: Named for the bleached appearance of the limestone fragments in the soil.
nature: A dry, stony field used for winter grazing.

name: The "Cairn" of the Dead
description: A small mound near Log na Marbh.
folklore: Believed to mark a specific grave from the 1584 O'Flaherty battle.
nature: A small heap of stones weathered by time.

name: An Pholl Dubh (Eochaill)
description: The Black Hole of Eochaill.
folklore: A deep swallow-hole in the limestone pavement.
nature: Surrounded by ferns and containing standing water.

name: An Gort MГѓВѓГ‚Віr (Fearann an Choirce)
description: The Large Field of the Hill Farm.
folklore: One of the most fertile fields on the island, used for potato cultivation.
nature: Level limestone soil with some clover.

name: An Buaile Glas
description: The Green Pasture.
folklore: A particularly fertile enclosure in the townland of CorrГѓВѓГ‚Вєch.
nature: Features thick, nutritious grass for cattle.

name: DГѓВѓГ‚Вєn ChonchГѓВѓГ‚Вєir (Fort of Conor)
description: A massive, elliptical stone fort commanding the islandГѓВўГ‚ВЂГ‚В™s highest pinnacle, characterized by monumental dry-stone terracing.
folklore: Associated with the Fir Bolg, the site served as the setting for a legendary banquet in 1857. Seventy members of the British Association, led by Sir William Wilde acting as cicerone, celebrated the island's antiquities here. Wilde addressed the party upon the structures, though islanders reportedly told "facetious stories" about the monuments' origins.
nature: The surrounding terrain is a "Vesuvian" landscape of massy, lava-colored limestone. The terraces provide a sense of "Alpine grandeur," with the grey rock appearing nearly molten in its geological intensity.

name: DГѓВѓГ‚Вєn Fearbhaigh
description: A prominent architectural cashel with formidable stone walls, strategically positioned to command the eastern approaches.
folklore: Regarded by antiquaries like Westropp as a primary "defence-line" for the island. Tradition holds that these walls were built to endure for millennia, only subsiding into the ocean after centuries of guarding against invaders from the mainland.
nature: Situated on a topographic scarp overlooking the Atlantic, the fort is constantly buffeted by the "boisterous ocean" and marine spray from the storm-beaten cliffs below.

name: Cill Cheannannach
description: A diminutive, early ecclesiastical ruin of monastic origin, noted for its primitive masonry and coastal proximity.
folklore: Dedicated to Saint Ceannannach. George Petrie identified this site as a monastic foundation, refuting earlier "Celtomaniac" theories that such structures were pagan fire-temples. It is celebrated as a landing place for saints who sought the "uncontaminated springs" of the islands.
nature: The site offers a "sea-parenthesis" view, where the horizon is framed by the grey limestone. It sits within a coastal environment where the land seems to merge with the Atlantic swell.

name: Tobar Cheannannach
description: A sacred holy well located near the shore, preserved as a significant site of traditional devotion.
folklore: A focal point for the "Aranite" pilgrimage, particularly during the patterns of the saints. John OГѓВўГ‚ВЂГ‚В™Donovan and George Petrie documented the siteГѓВўГ‚ВЂГ‚В™s continued status as a place for spiritual solace and historical preservation amidst the rocky landscape.
nature: Often found nestled within the "chinks" of the limestone pavement, the well is a rare source of freshwater in a landscape defined by parched, barren rock.

name: SyngeГѓВўГ‚ВЂГ‚В™s Cottage (Teach Synge)
description: A traditional thatched cottage that served as the residence and linguistic laboratory for playwright J.M. Synge.
folklore: Nicknamed "Ollscoil na Gaeilge" (The University of Irish), it became a shrine for the Gaelic League. Figures like PГѓВѓГ‚ВЎdraic Pearse and Fr. Eugene OГѓВўГ‚ВЂГ‚В™Growney stayed here. Lady Gregory, however, expressed a passionate "galling jealousy" of SyngeГѓВўГ‚ВЂГ‚В™s deep connection to the islanders, wishing to be "alone among the fishers."
nature: The cottage is integrated into the "labyrinth" of thousands of field-walls and limestone terraces that define the central townlands.

name: Cathaoir Synge (SyngeГѓВўГ‚ВЂГ‚В™s Chair)
description: A natural stone seat perched precariously on the edge of the western cliffs.
folklore: This site represents Synge's "matchless matching of place and person." Here, he observed island girls in their "dull red" petticoats, which he felt harmonized perfectly with the grey limestone. Lady GregoryГѓВўГ‚ВЂГ‚В™s own visits to these heights were marked by her envy of SyngeГѓВўГ‚ВЂГ‚В™s aesthetic intimacy with the island.
nature: Positioned above a vertical drop into the "boisterous ocean," the site is frequently obscured by heavy marine spray and the roar of the Atlantic.

name: Poll an tSГѓВѓГ‚В©ideГѓВѓГ‚ВЎin (The Puffing Hole)
description: A dramatic natural blowhole formed by the relentless marine erosion of the limestone strata.
folklore: Visitors often viewed this as an "awfully sublime" feature of the Romantic landscape. It represents the "primitive" power of the island that so fascinated 19th-century scholars and "sentimental tourists."
nature: Erosion has created a vertical shaft; during heavy swells, the Atlantic is forced through the rock, resulting in a towering plume of salt spray and air.

name: Baile an Mhothair / Baile an Teampaill / Baile an Lorgain
description: The primary, densely clustered townlands situated upon the limestone pavement.
folklore: In 1893, these villages hosted the Haddon and Browne Anthropometric Laboratory. The researchers observed the "Aranite" faceГѓВўГ‚ВЂГ‚В”long, oval, and well-markedГѓВўГ‚ВЂГ‚В”concluding the inhabitants were a "pure" race of primeval Gaelic stock.
nature: Newcomers often react with "consternation" at the "desert" appearance of these settlements, where houses are built directly onto the bare, lava-colored rock of the terraces.

name: Carraig an PhГѓВѓГ‚В­obaire (The PiperГѓВўГ‚ВЂГ‚В™s Rock)
description: A distinct limestone feature steeped in musical and subterranean lore.
folklore: Legend tells of a piper who entered the cave of "An Uamhain" on the neighboring island of Inis OГѓВѓГ‚В­rr. His music was reportedly heard traveling under the sound between the islands, eventually emerging from beneath this rock on Inis MeГѓВѓГ‚ВЎin.
nature: A singular landmark within the "interminable and disheartening" labyrinth of stone field-walls.

name: Leaba RГѓВѓГ‚ВіnГѓВѓГ‚ВЎin (Ronan's Bed)
description: A sacred grave site at the foot of a burial ground, traditionally used for curative rituals.
folklore: Islanders would sleep in the "leaba" on August 15th to seek cures. Folklore warns of a man who uprooted an elderberry bush here and was "smitten with a stroke." In 1947, Parish Priest Fr. Killeen controversially cleared the alder trees to allow for religious processions.
nature: Once characterized by a rare "forest" of alder trees in a landscape where such vegetation is virtually unknown. These trees grew wherever the saint was said to have walked.

name: An Aill BhrГѓВѓГ‚В­ste (The Broken Cliff)
description: A sharp vertical rock-face and carcair (slope) located near the old teachers' residence.
folklore: Features a "grotto" in a nook of the cliff containing a blue-and-white Madonna statue. Synge described the area as a "tapestry" of rambling roses and "old manГѓВўГ‚ВЂГ‚В™s beard," where the bright and the shadowy greenery met.
nature: A sanctuary for wildlife, including the goldcrest, whose bright crown appears as a "bright streak" amidst the "shadowy greenery" and rambling roses.

name: DГѓВєn Aonghasa
description: The best-known prehistoric fort on the Aran Islands, set at the edge of a 100-metre vertical cliff on Inis MГѓВіr. Four concentric drystone walls enclose roughly 6 hectares. Initial construction dates to c. 1100 BC (Late Bronze Age); the triple-wall defences were likely added around 500 BC. A cheval de frise of upright limestone spikes planted in the bedrock surrounds the outer walls.
folklore: The name means 'Fort of Aonghas', linking it to either the pre-Christian god Aengus or the mythical Fir Bolg king Aonghus mac ГѓВљmhГѓВіr. Irish mythology holds that the Fir Bolg retreated to the Aran Islands after their defeat by the Tuatha DГѓВ© Danann at the First Battle of Moytura, and that DГѓВєn Aonghasa was their chief stronghold. An Iron Age bird-head fibula (brooch) was discovered in a wall in 1839, the earliest physical relic recovered from the site.
nature: Clifftop karst landscape at 100m above the Atlantic. The cheval de frise is one of the best-preserved examples in Ireland or Britain. The surrounding plateau is classic Burren-type limestone pavement with grykes sheltering specialised flora.

name: Ireland West Airport Knock
description: Ireland's largest regional airport outside Dublin and Cork, built on bogland in County Mayo at an elevation of 168 metres вЂ” unusually high for any airport. Opened in 1986, it was conceived and fundraised entirely by Monsignor James Horan, the local parish priest, against near-universal scepticism from government and engineers. It serves as the main air gateway for pilgrims to Knock Shrine and tourists heading to Connemara and the Aran Islands.
folklore: Monsignor Horan raised the funds through parish collections and lobbying, calling the airport 'the miracle of the boglands'. He died in Lourdes in 1986, the very year it opened, never seeing its first scheduled flight. Locals say he went to his grave certain it would succeed.
nature: Set at 168 m above sea level on the north Mayo boglands, making it one of the highest-elevation commercial airports in Western Europe. The surrounding peatland is characteristic of the western Irish uplands.

name: Knock Shrine
description: Ireland's national Marian pilgrimage site, established after fifteen villagers reported a silent apparition of the Virgin Mary, Saint Joseph, and Saint John the Evangelist on the south gable of the parish church on the evening of 21 August 1879. Today the shrine attracts 1.5 million pilgrims annually and ranks alongside Lourdes and FГЎtima as one of Europe's major Marian sanctuaries. The complex includes five churches, the enclosed Apparition Chapel, basilica, museum, and 100 acres of pilgrim gardens.
folklore: On that wet August night, fifteen witnesses of all ages watched two silent figures in white light for about two hours вЂ” no words were spoken, no messages given. A subsequent ecclesiastical commission found their testimonies 'trustworthy and satisfactory'. Pope John Paul II visited on the centenary in 1979.
nature: The original apparition gable of the 19th-century parish church is now enclosed within the Apparition Chapel. The broader shrine grounds include chapels, a large open-air altar capable of hosting tens of thousands, and the annual August novena which is Ireland's largest Marian gathering.

name: Westport
description: One of Ireland's best-preserved planned Georgian towns, designed by architect James Wyatt in the late 18th century for the Browne family (Earls of Altamont). The town is built around the Carrowbeg River, which flows as a tree-lined canal through the main Mall. It is the main base for exploring Clew Bay, Croagh Patrick, and the islands of Clew Bay, and is consistently ranked among Ireland's most liveable towns.
folklore: Westport House is said to stand on the foundations of a castle once belonging to Grace O'Malley (GrГЎinne Mhaol), the 16th-century pirate queen of Connacht who ruled the seas around Clew Bay from her fleet of galleys. The dungeons of the current house are said to follow the layout of her original prison.
nature: Clew Bay, visible from the town, contains roughly 365 drumlins вЂ” one for each day of the year, according to local lore вЂ” formed by glacial deposition and now partially submerged. The bay is an important habitat for wading birds and seals.

name: Murrisk
description: A small village at the foot of Croagh Patrick on the southern shore of Clew Bay, and the traditional starting point for the pilgrimage climb of the mountain. The ruins of Murrisk Abbey (Augustinian, founded 1457) stand at the water's edge. The village is also the location of the National Famine Memorial bronze sculpture by John Behan.
folklore: Murrisk Abbey was founded by the O'Malley clan вЂ” the same family as the pirate queen Grace O'Malley вЂ” and several O'Malleys are buried in its grounds. The abbey was suppressed during the Reformation but its ruins retain fine carved stonework on the east window.
nature: The shoreline at Murrisk offers sweeping views across Clew Bay toward Achill Island. At low tide the tidal flats are rich in wading birds. The bay's drumlin islands are a distinctive post-glacial landscape.

name: National Famine Memorial
description: A large bronze sculpture by artist John Behan, unveiled by President Mary Robinson in 1997 on the shore of Clew Bay at Murrisk. The work depicts a coffin ship вЂ” the cramped, disease-ridden vessels that carried starving emigrants from Ireland during the Great Famine of 1845вЂ“52 вЂ” with skeletal figures tangled in the rigging, representing the hundreds of thousands who died on the crossing. It stands in view of Croagh Patrick, Ireland's holy mountain.
folklore: The 'coffin ships' were overcrowded and poorly provisioned; mortality rates on some crossings exceeded 30%. Many vessels departing from Westport and nearby ports were headed for Canada and the United States. The inscription at the base reads: 'Dedicated to the memory of all those who left these shores during the Great Famine 1845вЂ“1852.'
nature: The memorial stands metres from the water's edge on Clew Bay, with direct views toward Croagh Patrick rising behind it. The shoreline at this point is a shallow tidal estuary used by wading birds.

name: Croagh Patrick
description: Ireland's holiest mountain, a quartzite peak rising 764 metres above Clew Bay in County Mayo. Saint Patrick is said to have fasted on its summit for forty days in 441 AD. Every year on Reek Sunday (last Sunday in July), up to 25,000 pilgrims climb barefoot as an act of penance вЂ” a tradition continuous since the early medieval period. On a clear day the summit commands views of over 100 drumlins in Clew Bay and, on exceptional days, as far as the Twelve Bens of Connemara.
folklore: Patrick is said to have banished the snakes of Ireland from this summit вЂ” standing at the southern precipice, he rang his bell and every serpent and venomous creature cast itself over the cliff edge. Earlier still, the mountain was a site of the pre-Christian harvest festival of Lughnasadh, held in honour of the god Lugh.
nature: The mountain's quartzite cone is one of the most distinctive profiles in Ireland. The western flanks above 500 m are covered in scree; the summit plateau is bare rock. The upper slopes are an internationally recognised archaeological landscape, with evidence of Bronze Age ritual activity predating the Christian associations.

name: Doo Lough
description: A dark, glacially-carved lake set between the Mweelrea and Sheeffry Hill ranges in remote southwest Mayo, often called one of the most dramatic landscapes in Ireland. The Doolough Pass road above the lake's eastern shore is the site of the Doolough Tragedy of March 1849, when hundreds of starving Famine victims walked here in snow and rain seeking poor law relief вЂ” who were turned away. At least seven bodies were found on the roadside afterwards. An annual Famine Walk and a memorial cross at the lakeshore commemorate the event.
folklore: A stone monument beside the lake carries the inscription from Mahatma Gandhi: 'How can men feel themselves honoured by the humiliation of their fellow beings?' The words were chosen because Gandhi had cited the Irish Famine as an example of colonial violence. The annual walk in late April follows the same road the famine victims walked in 1849.
nature: Doo Lough (DГєloch, 'black lake') sits at around 30 m above sea level and is over 5 km long. It is extremely deep and oligotrophic, with dark peaty water draining from the bogs above. The surrounding valley is one of the least developed landscapes in Ireland, with Mweelrea (Connacht's highest mountain at 814 m) rising to the west.

name: Leenaun
description: A tiny village at the head of Killary Harbour, Ireland's only true fjord, where County Galway meets County Mayo. Leenaun achieved fame as the setting for Jim Sheridan's 1990 film 'The Field', starring Richard Harris, based on John B. Keane's play about land and identity in the west of Ireland. The village is surrounded by the Maumturk Mountains to the south and Ben Gorm to the north.
folklore: John B. Keane's 'The Field' reflects the deep Irish connection between land, memory, and identity forged during and after the Famine. A farmer who has rented, fertilised and tended a field for decades is outbid by an American вЂ” the play ends in violence. The film was shot in Leenaun and surroundings in 1989.
nature: Leenaun sits at the inner end of Killary Harbour. The Erriff River, which drains the Partry Mountains, enters the fjord here and is one of Ireland's best salmon rivers. The mountain walls on both sides rise steeply from sea level, giving the village a deeply enclosed, dramatic character.

name: Killary Harbour
description: Ireland's only fjord вЂ” a 16-kilometre-long glacially-carved sea inlet on the border of Counties Galway and Mayo. It reaches depths of over 45 metres at its centre. The southern shore is in County Galway; the northern shore, dominated by Mweelrea (Connacht's highest peak at 814 m), is in County Mayo. A mussel farm operates in the sheltered mid-reach. Boat tours run along the fjord from Leenaun from spring to autumn.
folklore: The Irish name An CaolГЎire Rua means 'the red fjord' вЂ” a reference some say to the reddish hue the water takes at sunset, and others to an old story about a battle whose blood turned the waters red. The fjord was used as a sheltered anchorage by smugglers running contraband between the west coast and the continent in the 18th century.
nature: A true fjord formed by glacial scouring during the last ice age, the harbour is deeper than much of the adjacent continental shelf. The mussel-farming operation takes advantage of the clean, fast-flowing tidal water. The mountain walls on both sides are nesting sites for peregrine falcons and ravens.

name: Maam Cross
description: A crossroads settlement in the heart of Connemara at the intersection of the N59 and the road to Maam valley, surrounded by bog, mountains, and the Maumturk range. The location is perhaps most famous as a principal filming site for John Ford's 1952 film 'The Quiet Man', starring John Wayne and Maureen O'Hara. The surrounding landscape of bog, lough, and mountain is quintessential Connemara.
folklore: John Ford brought John Wayne and Maureen O'Hara here to film 'The Quiet Man', using the area around Maam Cross and the nearby village of Cong as stand-ins for a romanticised Ireland. The film made this corner of Connacht internationally famous. The 'White O'Morn' cottage used in the film still stands nearby.
nature: The crossroads lies on the edge of the Maumturk Mountains at roughly 50 m above sea level, surrounded by blanket bog and oligotrophic loughs. The area is within the Connemara LIFE Natura 2000 zone, notable for its intact Atlantic bog systems and upland heath.

name: Carraroe Peninsula
description: A deeply indented peninsula in the Gaeltacht heartland of south Connemara, one of the most strongly Irish-speaking communities in Ireland. The Coral Strand (TrГЎ an DГіilГ­n) at the tip of the peninsula is formed almost entirely of maerl вЂ” a pink-white coralline algae вЂ” rather than sand, making it one of the most unusual beaches in Ireland. The landscape of low granite hills, numerous loughs, and Atlantic inlets is characteristic of the south Connemara 'drowned' coastline.
folklore: The entire peninsula is Irish-speaking (Gaeltacht); Irish is the everyday language of home, shop, and school. Local storytellers (seanchaГ­) kept oral traditions alive here long after they died out elsewhere.
nature: The Coral Strand is a Site of Community Importance under the EU Habitats Directive specifically for its maerl beds вЂ” the living alga forms delicate branched structures on the seabed and washes ashore over millennia. The surrounding coastline of south Connemara is a mosaic of bays, islands, and channels of international nature conservation importance.

name: Rossaveal Ferry Pier
description: The main ferry terminal for services to the Aran Islands from the Connemara mainland, located on the southern shore of Connemara at the end of the R336. Ferries from here reach Kilronan (Inishmore) in about 40 minutes вЂ” significantly faster than the Galway city route. The pier was developed specifically to serve the growing year-round Aran Island community and tourist traffic.
folklore: The fishing village of Rossaveal (An Ros Muc) is part of the south Connemara Gaeltacht. The surrounding coastline was historically a centre of traditional currach fishing, and the currachs still in use on the Aran Islands trace their design directly to the Connemara tradition.
nature: The pier opens onto Greatman's Bay (Cuan an Fhir MhГіir), a sheltered natural harbour used by fishing vessels and the Aran ferries. The bay is part of a European Special Area of Conservation for its seagrass beds and the harbour porpoise population using the bay.

name: Kilronan Pier
description: The main arrival point on Inishmore (Inis MГіr) and the island's commercial hub. The pier in Kilronan (Cill RГіnГЎin) receives all Aran Islands ferries from Rossaveal and Galway, as well as the island cargo boat. Behind the pier, Kilronan village has shops, cafГ©s, a pub, bike hire, and pony-trap operators вЂ” the entire island infrastructure radiates outward from this point.
folklore: Cill RГіnГЎin means 'church of RГіnГЎn', a reference to Saint RГіnГЎn Finn, an early Christian monk associated with Inishmore. The island has been inhabited for at least 3,000 years and Kilronan's harbour has been the main point of connection with the mainland for all of that time.
nature: The pier is one of the few sheltered landing points on the eastern side of Inishmore. Killeany Bay is a relatively calm, south-facing anchorage that provides protection from the prevailing westerlies. Grey seals are regularly seen hauled out on rocks near the pier entrance.

name: Seal Colony
description: A resident colony of grey Atlantic seals (Halichoerus grypus) on the northern shoreline of Inishmore, clearly signposted on the road between Kilmurvey and DГєn Aonghasa. At low tide, a dozen or more seals haul out on the flat limestone rocks and small beach, with the mountains of Connemara as a backdrop. A small shelter has been established for visitors at the cliff-top viewpoint.
folklore: The Irish west coast seals have long been associated with the mythological Selkies вЂ” shapeshifting creatures who are seals in the water and humans on land. Fishermen believed that harming a seal brought bad luck.
nature: Grey seals (Halichoerus grypus) are the larger of the two seal species native to Ireland. Adults can weigh up to 235 kg. The colony on Inishmore's north coast is resident year-round, using the sheltered limestone ledges for resting and pupping in autumn.

name: Kilmurvey Beach
description: A sheltered, Blue Flag sandy beach on the northern coast of Inishmore, a short walk from DГєn Aonghasa. The beach faces north toward the mountains of Connemara across a stretch of clear, shallow water with extraordinary visibility. It is one of the most scenic beaches on the Aran Islands and is rarely crowded. The limestone pavement surrounding the beach is characteristic of the island's karst landscape.
folklore: Kilmurvey (Cill Mhuirbhigh) takes its name from a church dedicated to Saint Muirbheal. The area around the beach was part of the estate of the Digby family, who were the main landlords of Inishmore in the 19th century.
nature: The beach is composed of clean white sand derived from fragmented shell and coralline material. The beach is adjacent to limestone pavement ('karren') with grykes harbouring rare plant species including Maidenhair Fern (Adiantum capillus-veneris) and Spring Gentian (Gentiana verna).

name: Bungowla
description: The westernmost village on Inishmore, lying about 9 km from Kilronan at the far end of the island's main road. Beyond Bungowla (Bun Gabhla), the island's road ends and the landscape opens onto the Atlantic. The village is the last settlement before the island's western cliffs, with views stretching unobstructed to the horizon. This is one of the most isolated and traditional communities on the Aran Islands, where Irish remains the everyday language.
folklore: Bungowla means 'the end of the fork' in Irish, referring to the branching point of paths at the western tip of the island. The village has maintained the strongest traditions of island life вЂ” including currach fishing and dry-stone wall building вЂ” long after they declined elsewhere.
nature: The western tip of Inishmore drops to the Atlantic via cliff faces and low rocky shores. The area is used by breeding seabirds including guillemots, razorbills, and choughs вЂ” the latter being the bird most associated with the Aran Islands.

name: DГєn DГєchathair
description: A large promontory stone fort on the southern cliffs of Inishmore near Killeany, also called the Black Fort. Walls up to 6 metres high and 5 metres wide surround an area that contains the ruins of several clochГЎns (beehive huts). A cheval de frise of jagged limestone spikes protects the landward approach. Coastal erosion has reduced the fort considerably since it was first recorded. Construction dates to between the 1st century BC and the 7th century AD.
folklore: Like the other great forts of Inishmore, DГєn DГєchathair is linked in legend to the Fir Bolg, the mythological pre-Celtic inhabitants of Ireland who are said to have retreated to the Aran Islands after their defeat in battle. The dark limestone of the cliffs and fort walls gives the site its 'black' name.
nature: The fort occupies a limestone promontory with sheer sea cliffs on three sides. The cliff faces below the fort are nesting sites for peregrine falcons, fulmars, and kittiwakes.

name: Gleann na nDeor
description: A small valley descending to the clifftops on the northern coast of Inishmore, whose name translates as 'Valley of Tears'. The name commemorates the departures of emigrants from the island during and after the Great Famine. When emigrant ships sheltered in Gregory Sound awaiting favourable winds, islanders could climb to this clifftop to see their loved ones on the ships one last time вЂ” too far away to call out to, too close to pretend they were already gone.
folklore: Tim Robinson, in his masterwork 'Stones of Aran: Pilgrimage', describes Gleann na nDeor as one of the most emotionally charged places on the island. The 'vale of tears' phrase comes from the old Catholic prayer 'Salve Regina', used to describe this world as a place of sorrow and exile вЂ” an apt double meaning for an island whose young people were leaving it, weeping, for the last time.
nature: The valley leads down through limestone pavement to the northern cliff edge, overlooking Gregory Sound and the open Atlantic. The clifftops here are carpeted in spring by wildflowers typical of the Aran Islands вЂ” Spring Gentian, Mountain Avens, Wild Thyme вЂ” growing directly in grykes in the bare limestone.

name: Puffing Holes
description: Two large and one small circular blowholes on the limestone plateau at the southeastern tip of Inishmore, beyond the village of Cill Г‰inne. When Atlantic swells push into the sea caves below the plateau, compressed air erupts upward through these natural vents in spectacular geysers of white water and spray. Access requires a cross-country walk across bare limestone pavement from Cill Г‰inne village.
folklore: Na Poill SeideГЎin ('the puffing holes') are known locally as a signal of incoming storms вЂ” the intensity of the spray is said to forecast how severe the weather will be. Fishermen traditionally read the blowholes before deciding whether to put to sea.
nature: The blowholes are formed where sea caves eroded by wave action have punched through the thin limestone plateau above. The flat karst pavement surrounding them is one of the most intact examples of this habitat type in Ireland, designated as a Special Area of Conservation.

name: DГєn ChonchГєir
description: The largest prehistoric stone fort on Inis MeГЎin, occupying the highest point of the island at about 60 metres above sea level. The oval cashel has an inner defensive wall reaching over 8 metres wide; originally over 14,000 tonnes of stone would have formed the inner wall alone. The fort's position gives commanding views of all three Aran Islands, Galway Bay, and the Connemara mountains.
folklore: The fort is named after ConchГєr, a semi-legendary king of the Aran Islands. J.M. Synge, who spent several summers on Inis MeГЎin between 1898 and 1902 gathering material for his plays, described sitting in the fort and feeling 'the full force of the spirit of the island'.
nature: DГєn ChonchГєir crowns the limestone ridge of Inis MeГЎin. The surrounding plateau hosts a limestone grassland flora typical of the Burren, including Spring Gentian, Bloody Cranesbill, and several species of orchid.

name: An TrГЎ
description: The main beach on Inis OГ­rr (the smallest Aran Island), a sweep of clean white sand on the northeastern coast sheltered by the island's small harbour. An TrГЎ ('The Strand') faces northeast across Galway Bay, with views of the Burren limestone cliffs on the Clare coast. Inis OГ­rr is the smallest and most compact of the three Aran Islands, with the beach at the centre of island social life.
folklore: The island looks across to the Burren coast of Clare, and its early Christian community maintained close ties with the monastic settlements on that coast. Saint CaomhГЎn (Kevin), patron of Inis OГ­rr, is said to be buried in the medieval church of Teampall ChaomhГЎin, which is partially buried in sand dunes at the southern edge of the beach.
nature: The beach is composed of crushed shell and carbonate sand, characteristically white. The sheltered bay is a nursery area for juvenile fish and used by harbour porpoise. The sand dunes behind the beach are a nationally important habitat, with a diverse grassland flora including Pyramidal Orchid and Lady's Bedstraw.

name: Clonmacnoise
description: One of Ireland's most important early medieval monastic sites, founded in 544 AD by Saint CiarГЎn on the banks of the River Shannon in County Offaly. At the crossing of the ancient east-west road (Slighe MhГіr) with Ireland's great river, Clonmacnoise became one of the foremost centres of Christian scholarship, art, and manuscript production in Europe. The site includes the ruins of a cathedral, eight churches, two round towers, three High Crosses, and over 200 early medieval grave slabs.
folklore: Saint CiarГЎn died of plague only seven months after founding the community, at around 33 years old вЂ” the same age as Christ. Vikings raided Clonmacnoise at least nine times between 795 and 1012. The Book of the Dun Cow, one of Ireland's oldest manuscripts in Irish, was compiled here around 1100.
nature: The monastery stands on a low ridge above the Shannon floodplain вЂ” the Callows вЂ” which floods seasonally and supports one of Ireland's largest inland concentrations of wintering wildfowl, including internationally important numbers of Whooper Swan and Greenland White-fronted Goose.

name: Kilmainham Gaol
description: A former prison in Kilmainham, Dublin, now one of Ireland's most visited museums, operated by the Office of Public Works. Built in 1796 and operational until 1924, Kilmainham Gaol held the leaders of almost every Irish rebellion from 1798 to the War of Independence. The leaders of the 1916 Easter Rising вЂ” Pearse, Connolly, Clarke, MacDonagh, Mac Diarmada, Plunkett, Ceannt вЂ” were executed by firing squad in the prison's stone-breakers' yard between 3 and 12 May 1916.
folklore: Joseph Plunkett married his fiancГ©e Grace Gifford in the prison chapel just hours before his execution. James Connolly, wounded in the fighting, was executed tied to a chair because he could not stand. The gaol's East Wing, visited by Oscar Wilde's mother, was said to be the most oppressive building in Ireland.
nature: The gaol is a striking example of Victorian penitentiary architecture, designed on the panopticon principle with radiating cell wings. The East Wing's central atrium is filled with natural light from a glazed roof, contrasting with the original 18th-century West Wing of dark, damp cells.
